output: Mr. President, dear colleagues, I once again ask your pardon for my delay.
This year marks the European Year of Intercultural Dialogue.
The involvement of all and the recognition that education, knowledge, and the interweaving of different cultures with one another are crucial is already a given, taken for granted.
For this reason, multiple initiatives linked to this year have been carried out by the various areas of action and intervention.
In this context, the Erasmus Mundus programme also plays its part, and under this new perspective, a new regulation is already in place.

The Erasmus Mundus programme constitutes an important contribution to the creation of centers of excellence in the EU, which will in some measure help to sustain the brain drain out of the European Union, as well as represent a significant role in

in promoting European values among nationals of third countries who come here to study and find in the old continent a cultural and linguistic diversity unmatched, which functions as an attractive and distinctive pole compared to models existing elsewhere in the world.
However, dialogue and interculturality have suffered greatly in order to survive.

The problems associated with student Erasmus Mundus visas, along with the frequent difficulties in obtaining and renewing them, often result in students living in a state of semi-illegality within the European Union, as they attend master's programs on tourist visas, and sometimes even face harassment.
It is of utmost relevance and urgency to seek a solution that ensures solidity, transparency, and effectiveness in the issuance of pre-student visas.
Moreover, knowledge of the language that enables cultural appreciation and mutual coexistence, beyond strictly academic proficiency, is crucial; thus, we can guarantee this premise absolutely.
To conclude, a word of thanks to Deputy Sarnez for the way she has conducted the entire process and for the balanced report she has produced.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, authorization for the granting of state aid for the modernization and renewal of fishing fleets in the outermost regions should either be possible only until the end of 2009 or should remain in force indefinitely.
The specific characteristics of fishing activities in these regions justify that such aid may be granted beyond 2009 without causing any significant harm to the marine environment.
State aid for the modernization and renewal of the fleets of the outermost regions over an indefinite period does not mean permanent and internal aid.
would be immediately halted if the studies and assessments conducted by the Scientific Council and the Regional Advisory Boards indicated such a need.
This is the solution that would best suit the very specific realities of fisheries in the overseas regions.
The same reasons that justify for fishermen of each RUP an exclusive fishing reserve up to seeding also justify a differentiated approach regarding assistance for fleet modernization and renewal.
Fortunately, within the Fisheries Committee, the best solutions were found that can address the specific characteristics of fisheries in the outermost regions.

Mr. President, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner Almunia, Mr. President of the Eurogroup, I would like to congratulate not only the rapporteurs but also the Commission for the initiative in producing this text, the assessment of the past ten years of the Economic and Monetary Union.
It is a strategic document, and this analysis was essential.
The euro, as has been said here today, is an undeniable success.

From Lisbon to Helsinki, from Dublin to Bratislava, the euro has demonstrated resilience and solidity even amid the severe turbulence we have witnessed, and it is clearly important that the boundaries of this club be expanded.
But the times we live in daily test the solidity of the European project in many other dimensions.
The euro, as I said, is a central instrument for the functioning of the financial system.
However, the Commission's own text clearly points to the fundamental conclusion that neither the growth of the real economy nor convergence, in social or spatial terms, has accompanied this success.
On the contrary, the Commission clearly concludes that disparities worsened sharply during the decade of the single currency's operation.
This discrepancy affects regions of my country, particularly northern Portugal, as well as other regions in member states.

The impacts of the single monetary policy, particularly due to the very high exchange rate applied, have affected more severely those regions most exposed to international competition and the most export-oriented regions.
Today, the crisis resulting from the deregulation of financial markets violently impacts the real economy and further worsens the situation of many citizens and many regions.

These regions—those exposed to international competition, those dependent on small and medium-sized enterprises, and those where access to credit is most critical—are currently suffering from this process and risk even falling into depression.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. President of the Eurogroup, the success of the euro depends on the trust that European citizens place in it.
The most powerful European countries have already begun the process of revitalizing their national economies.
We have, in particular, the case of initiatives concerning the automotive industry.
But in Europe, it is more than that.
It must be considerably more than a more or less coordinated sum of national policies.
The time has come to transform the euro into a central instrument for Europe, supporting not only a strong currency but also a robust real economy, and to ensure the proper functioning of the euro.
of mechanisms that ensure the central objective of achieving social and regional convergence, as this is at the heart, at the very core, of the European project.
Debates about the coordination of economic policies in the euro area today have an undeniable and reinforced impact and relevance.
But coordinating national policies is not enough.
More is needed.

Having a Lisbon strategy, which is a compilation of national initiatives, and a stability and growth pact, which differentially constrains the various countries using it and which they must comply with, are limitations to which we must add the limited budget at our disposal.
Crises generate opportunities.
Monetary union can only be sustained through a convergence of well-being among the member countries, regardless of their citizens and irrespective of where they were born.
What other means would we have to mobilize citizens toward the single currency?

The Commission must live up to its own diagnosis, which is why we are eagerly awaiting the concrete proposals that the Commission will bring to this Plenary session on 26 November and present to European citizens.

It is essential that, starting from this crisis, a new phase of the Union be launched, in which the consolidation and strength of the financial system are accompanied by genuine prosperity grounded in cohesion and economic citizenship as well.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, this oral question submitted to the European Commission by Gerardo Gagliotti, on behalf of the Parliament's Committee on Regional Development, is fully justified and pertinent.

In fact, the reasons prompting the Council to block the reform of the Solidarity Fund must be clearly stated, inasmuch as it is unable to reach a common position that would allow the legislative process to proceed.
It is difficult to discern the motives underlying the Council's position, except perhaps for petty financial reasons.
Does the Council not want a faster and more immediate response to the natural disasters that occur?
I do not want the Council to give this kind of swift response to other types of disasters, such as major industrial accidents, terrorist attacks, or serious public health situations.

It is important that these issues receive a clear response, just as it is important to clarify what the European Commission thinks about this situation and whether it intends to take any measures aimed at unblocking the legislative process.
The value of solidarity must also be fully realized in this area.
The citizens would not understand at all if we were not to act this way.
I mean that I wished to protest against the statement made by Mrs. Lulling, which seeks to condition the votes of Members of this House, and such conditioning makes no sense.

The interpretation you are making is one that we do not share, and it is in this sense that I would ask my colleagues—those who know what they are dealing with—to recognize that we are defending other interests, namely those of public health, the interests of citizens, and the interests of the countries that genuinely support the report.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, for two months the political and media agenda has been dominated by the financial and economic crisis.
The referendum in Ireland or the difficulties in the Czech Republic regarding the Treaty of Lisbon are hardly mentioned in the media.

Don't you think that the Commission's information and communication strategy should now be reinforced by the need and urgency of the Lisbon Treaty's entry into force, also as a political response to address and overcome the crisis we are experiencing?
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, at that moment the world's powers have the international financial system at their disposal.
They were looking for a hand detail, and it was high time they found it.
And therefore, perhaps it is time for us to address right now another problem about which we have heard for decades.
For decades we have heard this song of the shameful plundering of resources, how many times, as now once again in the Congo, over the blood of populations.

I believe it is also time to pursue these companies, to target this abhorrent trade, and to freeze the accounts through which the proceeds of this shameful exploitation of minerals—achieved at the cost of blood and suffering of local populations—circulate and are deposited.
I don't understand why these companies are not listed under the same heading under which we have already compiled a blacklist of terrorist organizations.
And I fail to understand why the international community is unable to pursue these individuals who, in truth, are not entrepreneurs but genuine criminals undermining regional and global security.
I encourage the French Presidency and the Commission to launch an international initiative in this regard.
We have heard here today on several occasions that a crisis normally generates an opportunity, and this is indeed the case.
And if we wish to seize the opportunity that lies before us, the agenda is easy to understand.
First of all, we know, at the level of the European Union, the need we have to introduce mechanisms of transparency, regulation, and supervision in financial markets.
And it is not necessary for Commissioner Macri to launch further consultations, nor is it required for the Presidency to analyze and invoke additional studies.
There are further proposals; one need only look at those promptly submitted by the socialists, many of which were later taken up by this Parliament.
Secondly, internationally, Europe cannot occupy an international space in a passive manner.
It must be an active agent in building the new international order; it must champion a new architecture that leaves no black holes in the system, such as tax havens are today.
Thirdly, today we have heard you, Mr President of the Commission, say that there is reluctance in coordinating strategies at the level of the various Member States.
It is true that, if we had not heard for so long from many members of the Commission that markets self-regulate, that the state should stay out.
that financial markets had no impact on growth or employment, perhaps there would be a more active and engaged spirit of solidarity at the level of different countries.
But today the proposal before us is merely a first step. If compared to the programs of China and the United States, on the other hand, it
does not sufficiently clearly define what the objective is, and the objective must be employment; and thirdly, because a European programme cannot be merely a sum of national agendas.
Today is the time for us to show European citizens that Europe is a network of economic rights that safeguards them during times of crisis, in terms of
solidarity, growth program, and support for small and medium-sized enterprises that at this moment are not receiving the results of the measures implemented at the banking level.
The real economy is precisely this, and we are here as Europeans to show citizens that Europe is here too, and above all, because of them. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, it is with particular satisfaction that I stand before you to present the work accomplished, work carried out jointly by all of us in this initiative report.
For me and for all those who contributed to the progression of this process, we can say that we have done our work.

to all, particularly the shadow rapporteurs from the various political groups who made great efforts to reach agreements for the future, to the regional development staff always available, notably Miguel Tel Cremades and Elisa da Fara,

to the staff of the DG PSE, to Lilla and Petros, to the European Commission, which, always represented by the many different directorates-general required by such an outstanding report, closely and continuously followed the work and made considerable efforts to ensure its successful outcome.
To my office, and particularly to Joana Benziño, my sincere thanks on behalf of a more harmonious and structured development for coastal regions and tourism across the European Union.
The 27 member states of the European Union together have over 89 kilometers of coastal areas.
It presents a variety of very distinctive characteristics that, depending on its location, are marked by cosmopolitanism, if we were talking about cities such as Lisbon, Copenhagen, Stockholm, or

the difficulties of being located in peripheral or ultraperipheral regions, facing challenges in connectivity to major urban centers or experiencing accelerated desertification contexts, such as the Algarve, Liguria, the Canary Islands, or Madeira.
In one way or another, everyone identifies with the concept of coast and coastal area and daily experiences the advantages and disadvantages associated with them.
According to data available up to 2010, approximately 75% of humanity will live in coastal areas. These areas
According to the circuits and their interrelations, we define the 50 kilometers in a straight line from the coastline to the inland areas.
Yes, regions—and not detached maritime strips—within an integrated perspective that these matters so urgently require, and that our ancestors would also endorse.
Here, this entire population will concentrate in search of opportunities and economic synergies, and in many cases, will expect these to revolve primarily around tourism.
Thus, the evident need for a pragmatic and integrated understanding of tourism's impact on coastal areas seized our minds and prompted us to take action.

In the current context of financial crisis, in which the impact on the real economy becomes increasingly unavoidable, tourism emerges as a sector with enormous potential to be severely affected, directly or indirectly.

Regions, which for their development have an overwhelming or total dependence on this, see their activities threatened and look to the future with suspicion, particularly because tourism is not currently a competence of the European Union.
However, coordinated measures can and should be taken, and the spirit in which the Treaty of Lisbon was framed reflects this same tendency.
However, waiting until the moment the Treaty enters into force to begin acting is like waiting until the stage when we will weep over spilled milk.
The political and tourist activity present, along with the fragile reality of the regions dependent on it, demands from us urgent and effective action.

The fact that these areas are structurally dependent on tourism as a driver of employment, despite often providing seasonal jobs, means that this labor-intensive activity cannot be overlooked in a context of pressure on urban areas and unemployment.

For this very reason, this report, which was already of the utmost opportunity and urgency when the Committee on Regional Development of this House decided to produce it, must now be regarded as an immediate priority for the European Commission and the European Council.

Priority yes to the numerous initiatives it encompasses, as well as those that express its explicit support and have already been conveyed by other institutions, alongside the measures adopted in the emergency plan

of the Commission, among which I highlight the review of the Globalisation Adjustment Fund, in which issues related to this sector and the current shock it is experiencing must obviously be addressed.

It is crucial to ensure the development of new sectors of the economy in these coastal areas, guaranteeing their social and environmental sustainability and promoting the effective integration of different sectoral policies, such as the maritime sector, transport,

energy, the cohesion instruments already on the ground, the new quality products policy incorporated by the CAP review through the announcement of the EFSC report, and the new tourism products in these coastal areas, taking into account their crucial contribution to the European economy.
The implementation of an appropriate and holistic vision for this policy must become a reality in the near term in the European Union.

And finally, Mr. President, only through the clear coordination of these instruments and swift, effective action involving all relevant partners already on the ground can we ensure that the European Union will have a sustainable and future-oriented coastal tourism sector.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, Madam Minister, eJustice is an important step towards access to law, justice, and courts.
I would like to commend the initiative, congratulate the rapporteur, and thank the French Presidency.

The EJSTIS was one of the projects discussed, and Portugal, during its presidency, called for leadership in developing and implementing a pilot project that will enable all citizens to access services from other Member States more easily, more affordably, more comfortably, and in their own language.
This multilingual portal should be designed to serve citizens and businesses seeking legal assistance and basic legal advice regarding cross-border legal issues.

The E-Justice initiative must be coordinated and overseen by a responsible unit, which will also coordinate the contributions from the various member states and ensure their alignment with this responsible unit.
Justice will serve the citizen and strengthen our democracy.
I welcome the launch of the portal in December 2009.
Mr. President, Madam President, ladies and gentlemen colleagues, I congratulate the work of Carlos Coelho, of the honourable Member Martin Rühi, and of the other Members.
In December 2004, the Council adopted a regulation establishing standards for the security features and biometric data in passports and travel documents issued by Member States.
Now, we have had the need to take further steps toward combating child abduction and trafficking.
The use of passports by children, in accordance with the principle of one person, one passport, may be the fundamental means of overcoming this difficult and important struggle.
In my country, Portugal, collecting digital fingerprints from children starting at the age of six is already a practice with a long-standing tradition, which perhaps is why it causes me no objection.
As a defender of the European cause, I believe that, in this matter, harmonization is important.
It reassures me to know that Member States, such as Portugal, which have long established the minimum age at six years for collecting fingerprints, will not need to amend their national legislation.
It should be emphasized that the security of passports, which is strengthened today, does not consist solely in the existence of the passport itself.

The passport, by itself, represents an increase in security level, ranging from the submission of required documents for passport issuance, through the collection of biometric data, to verification and matching at border control points.
This report represents another step toward affirming the rights of the individual and ensuring their safety.
Mr. President, first of all, I would like to welcome the rapporteur Alexander Lambsdorff and the shadow rapporteurs, with particular recognition for Barbara Weiler.
Dear colleagues, I believe it is essential that we move forward toward the integration of national defense markets and even the strategic coordination of their production.

We must effectively progress with the necessary specifics and precautions toward applying the fundamental rules of the internal market to defense industries, increasing transparency in tenders organized and contracts signed involving the Member States of the European Union.
At the same time, we must help create the conditions that enable European equipment and products to achieve greater competitiveness in global markets.

It seems to me important that, through this directive, progress can be ensured in the legal framework governing public contracts in the fields of defence and security, advancing the construction of the internal market.

internal, while obviously respecting the interests and rights of the Member States, particularly in the field of security and, notably, those manufacturers of weapons, ammunition, and security equipment, such as my own country.

I would also highlight the provisions that facilitate easier access for small and medium-sized enterprises to this market, particularly through the improvement of subcontracting rules and the prospect of advancing with greater impact and depth in

industrial for the creation of a genuine European defense market, which could also contribute to strengthening research and development, not only in defense industries but also across a broad range of European industries benefiting from advancements in defense industries.
To conclude, I believe one can refer to the obvious linkage between defense industries and the Union's foreign policies.
But I would like to refer, regarding the DETA, to a recent statement, translating the classic Romanian proverb: "If you want peace, prepare for war."

The European Union, as it is nearly a peaceful superpower, we refer to these industries as defense industries and not war industries, because we seek peace and not war, and therefore we also need defense industries.
Mr. Vice-President Barrot, I agree with you, I agree with you, albeit with dissatisfaction.
In fact, we must be dissatisfied with the overall set of measures, which are extensive.
The truth is that irregular immigration continues to Europe, human trafficking persists, and there are areas where there is much
With regard to immigration, particularly in the Mediterranean region towards Italy, France has advanced a set of important bilateral cooperations, but other Member States have not followed suit.
It is necessary that the Commission continue without losing enthusiasm in this policy of cooperation and assistance to the United States.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, I will try to present the issues in a very concise manner.
First point: the issue regarding the European Union's special monitoring mechanism for testing, which ended, as stated here, on December 31, 2008.
Madam Commissioner, timely and routine publication of statistical data on imports, exports, and prices is lacking, in contrast to what occurs in the United States.
Without these data, the European Union is unable to respond to potential unfair practices, and the Commission itself becomes unable to define its strategy.
I drew his attention to this, in the same manner as other colleagues have already done.
Secondly, producing within the European Union increasingly requires compliance with safety standards, social standards, and environmental standards.
The RITES, for example, is a recent initiative that imposes even more constraints upon us.
It is important that the Commission has a clear strategy that is perceptible and capable of being monitored, so that imported products meet identical requirements.
How are these aspects being properly addressed in the ongoing free trade agreements?
And regarding Meidine, can Meidine actually help resolve this issue?
Third point: The European Union launched an economic recovery plan to address the current crisis.
What role will commercial policy play in this context?

At this moment, what is the Commission preparing to do when a series of other countries, as is the case with China, increasingly proliferate the launch of non-tariff barriers, preventing our European imports from accessing the Chinese market?

What does it propose regarding the updating or adjustment of the Globalisation Fund, as well as the available support under the structural funds frameworks to improve the immediate situation of the European textile industry?
Final question, last but not least, in fact, will the crisis we are going through finally make the Commission sensitive to the devastating impact on the European economy of an overvalued euro?
How can the Commissioner sensitize, to conclude, her colleagues and the institutions responsible for European monetary policy to rebalance their monetary system?
For us, it is clear that we must applaud Obama's decision—the decision to close Guantánamo, to prohibit torture, illegal interrogations, and the suspension of military trials.
Nevertheless, dear colleague, who is no longer present, Portugal and the Portuguese socialist government have no sense of guilt whatsoever.
The Portuguese socialist government did not cooperate with the Bush administration, quite the opposite.

It was precisely now, with the Portuguese administration, with the Portuguese Government, and with the initiative taken by Minister Luiz Amado, that the possibility and duty of the European Union and democratic countries to welcome and receive those detained without formal charges at Guantánamo were triggered.

That is truly what matters, that is what should be highlighted as a model for the remaining member states of the European Union to follow. It is also crucial that tomorrow the resolution be approved by all the political parties represented in this Chamber, so that, united, the decision may carry greater weight.
Mr. President, thank you very much to colleague Napolitano for the excellent report.

Fourteen years after Barcelona and five years after the first Euro-Mediterranean Parliamentary Assembly, we now have the Union for the Mediterranean, endowed with economic power, parliamentary authority, civil society, and heads of government collectively making decisions.
All with the desire to do more and better in a territory where 720 million citizens represent a potential for development and peace for the world.
Following them solely from the economic and business perspective is a clear mistake.

Responding to growth crises naturally requires political response, but responding to humanitarian crises, emergency situations, and military crises—such as the one experienced at the end of 2008 and beginning of 2009—is crucial.
Politicians and citizens must provide answers to the world's problems.
The world demanded answers, and months later, we are still, citizens and politicians alike—particularly on a platform such as the Union for the Mediterranean—delaying our response.
I hope, at the very least, this moment has served to learn and progress, as only those who make mistakes truly learn; however, advancing in accelerating the response is absolutely crucial.
The interest in adopting this proposal for a directive essentially lies in its potential to stimulate local economic activity and support social initiatives.

The application of a reduced rate is of great importance in the housing sector, both in construction and renovation, enabling Member States that adopt it to lower the associated costs for the end user.

The provision of local services, particularly by small enterprises, may also benefit from this reduction in the cost of living, ensuring and generating more and better employment by encouraging the consumption of goods and services, thus stimulating the economy itself.

The restoration activity, essential for the proper functioning of local economies and the very growth of tourism, can thus gain significantly at the local level through this reduction in VAT taxation.
These services, dear colleagues, should not circulate in the European internal market, which will help reduce the reserves of certain Member States already noted in the Council's plan.
Therefore, I cannot agree with extending this reduced rate to equipment, for whatever pretext.

The prudent manner in which this proposal should be handled in the European Parliament must not provide an excuse for governments that have certain reservations regarding the European Commission's proposal to actually exercise the veto power still granted to them under European legislation in fiscal matters.
Thus, here one must prudently reject changes of great significance, as they only hinder acceptance of the text.
As already mentioned here, not only is the perfect the enemy of the good, but he who wants everything loses everything.
This draft directive is important not only for strengthening local economies, but also for aligning with the ambitions repeatedly expressed by various Member States.

Let us therefore support the Commission's proposal as it was presented; indeed, that was precisely the intent of the vote, as I understand it, of the Commission on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection.

I commend the work of the rapporteurs and appeal for the approval of this proposal exactly as submitted by the Commission and as voted on by the Parliamentary Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection.
I was given the floor to disagree with my colleague Edith Estrella, and indeed, that was precisely what motivated me to speak. It was when she caricatured the intentions of the Czech Presidency.
No one wants to send women home. The problem is that there are women who want to or would like to stay at home.

There are even associations that seek to defend them—women who are ignored, despised, discriminated against simply for wanting to dedicate themselves to their families, and yet we do not listen to them.
And this is a matter of freedom, a matter of the freedom of choice that the left so often invokes, yet forgets here.
This concerns whether or not to respect couples' freedom of organization.
It is about enabling those who wish to do so to enjoy a better quality of life, improved fatherhood, and improved motherhood.
That is what our society also needs to do.
We won't get there by resolving people's issues of birth rates and happiness through prejudice.
We resolved it through policies tailored to reality and people's spontaneous desires.
We are not going there as an opposition to the State and to the market.
Let us now take a fair look at the family.
Mr. President, Commissioner, fellow colleagues, the difficult situation at Quimonda has been exacerbated by the onset of the financial and economic crisis.
The Portuguese government has done everything it considers appropriate and useful to help resolve the situation.
We would like the Bavarian government also to be able to act with great sense of responsibility in this area.
Saving the Munich facilities will have a decisive impact on the units in Vila do Conde and Dresden.

The Portuguese minister himself, Manuel Pinho, stated the significant interest in making this company, so important for Portugal and Europe, viable, noting that Aquimonda could become a globally competitive enterprise.
Mr. President, the Portuguese Government will continue its efforts toward a solution that ensures the company's viability.
We hope that the German federal and state governments will effectively commit themselves to resolving this issue.
As for me, I would like to re-emphasize the strategic importance of maintaining this type of industry within European territory.
that no national or state executive should make the mistake of allowing this company to close facilities and eliminate jobs within the territory of the Union.

Dear colleagues, we understand the need to assist in the restructuring of major groups in the automotive industry, but the funds of the Union and the energies of the European Commission cannot be exhausted by such aid.

Mr. President, dear Commissioner, we appeal to Commissioner Vladimir Spilá—whom we are linked to through both professional collaboration and political solidarity—and to President Barroso, who will never forget that he is our compatriot and that of a significant portion of Quimonda’s workers—to commit the Commission to supporting Quimonda, overcoming any potential communication shortcomings.

We should emphasize, before concluding, that the Portuguese President, Cavaco Silva, recently stated in Germany that there was renewed hope regarding Quimonda, and also highlighted the importance—already underscored by colleagues—of the economic sector in which Quimonda operates.
We will take into account, as soon as the Portuguese government indicates its willingness to support Quimonda with whatever is necessary, bearing in mind the country's economic significance.
Ladies and gentlemen, let us help save Quimonda—the time is urgent.

Madam President, Commissioner, we have been discussing the Lisbon Strategy for years, and the times we are living in may not be the most suitable for talking about the Lisbon Strategy, but we undoubtedly need a strategy.
in a strategy that responds to the difficulties and challenges that the current crisis presents to us.
And that is what we also expect from the Commission.

It is essential that the Commission does not distance itself from this issue and, on the contrary, has the capacity to coordinate actions with the governments of Portugal and the governments of Germany and the German-speaking countries.

It is essential to bear in mind that this is an industry, as already stated here, of importance to Europe due to its quality and value, the research it carries out, and the environmental quality associated with it as well, and it is essential that the Commission does not distance itself.
I endorse the invitation made by my colleague Pineda for your Commissioner and the Commission to visit Portugal.

It is essential that the Commission keeps in mind that, at this moment, Europeans are looking to Europe and expect responses from European authorities. They need to feel that European authorities are close at hand.
European citizens do not understand a Europe that washes its hands; they want a Europe that dares to roll up its sleeves and get to work, helping to overcome difficulties. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, I join in paying tribute to Jammeh, but I have requested the floor to salute your President for the statement he made regarding the European Day of Remembrance for the Victims of Terrorism.
It was through my proposal that, five years ago, we adopted this orientation, which the Council formally endorsed during a meeting on March 25, following the tragic attacks in Madrid.
But my intervention was an appeal.

The Parliament has always remained faithful to commemorating this date; however, unfortunately, this date has still not received, across the European institutions and the Member States, the visibility it should have.
I believe that commemorating this date is one of the most important tools we have to honor the victims, as its President did, but also to raise the level of public awareness.
I know there are some celebrations in Madrid today, but nothing more.
Therefore, my appeal to the Commission, to the Czech Presidency, is that all Member States duly commemorate this date in the future.
(FEL) Ladies and gentlemen, my dear colleagues, the crisis is deeper than we expected and unemployment will be greater than we predicted.
There are strong reasons to believe the anticipated European stimulus is insufficient, but it is already clear at this stage that it is taking far too long to reach citizens.
The Parliament's position is strong and clear; it has been so, and I hope it will continue to be.
Our objective is to maintain employment and create new jobs, with cohesion and solidarity, both territorial and social.
And at this moment of crisis, citizens cannot accept an Europe that fails to respond to their needs, an Europe that is powerless in the face of the challenges they are experiencing.
What is the Parliament going to ask of the Commission?

Naturally, through these reports, it requests that you coordinate national interventions, asks you to use all available means at your disposal to act, and provides you with all necessary conditions, as a budgetary entity, for this to occur.

Ask him to launch a clear European initiative on employment, and tell him it is important to have a timetable-driven agenda to implement measures regulating the financial market, as well as to ensure credit reaches the real economy.
But what is being asked of the council?
It is requested of the Council, above all, to rediscover the political will that lies at the heart of the European project's construction.
The European Union is competition, but it is also cohesion and solidarity.
And there cannot be a single market without this guarantee of solidarity and cohesion.
That is precisely why we all delegated, at the European level, the national competences we previously held before joining this project. Thank you very much.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, anyone visiting sub-Saharan Africa readily recognizes in the majority of countries the immense weakness of their health systems and the extremely negative impact this weakness has on the lives and health of the populations these services should serve.
The indicators continuously disseminated internationally ceaselessly confirm this.
And to that extent, it is absolutely disturbing to consider that simple gestures and practices, not particularly elaborate or costly, might be sufficient to save many lives.

European financial support may be crucial in this regard, and we must never lose sight of how cooperation in the field of health is truly strategic, directly addressing not only one of the Millennium Development Goals but also many of them.

The Court of Auditors considered that community funding for the health sector had not increased since 2000 in proportion to its overall development assistance, despite the commitments made by the Commission regarding the Millennium Development Goals and the public health crisis in sub-Saharan Africa.
It is requested.

And acknowledged, once again quoting the Commission, that it had significantly contributed, through its funding, to establishing the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, but had not given the same attention to strengthening health systems, which was expected to be one of its priorities.
(FEL) Final quotation.

According to the Court, this has happened again because the Commission lacked sufficient experience in health matters to ensure that funding provided to this sector was being used in the most effective manner.
Finci that.
This therefore represents a significant challenge directly issued by the Court of Auditors to the European Commission, which I support.
On our part, I wish to reiterate this same challenge, now grounded in the objectivity of these data and this assessment.
Health services are already part of our development assistance priorities, but they should increasingly be so, and deserve increased funding.

Optimizing the way assistance is delivered, keeping in mind the seemingly opposing needs of coordination in its management and proximity to beneficiary populations, is providing a service that can save many lives.
The European Commission cannot but respond positively to this request, and it is in this sense that I urge it.
My colleague Bowis, not long ago, delivered a moving intervention here and managed to give faces—human faces—to the cold, dry figures presented by the Court of Auditors.
The challenge for us, Commissioner, is for our cooperation to be able to instill in those very faces a look of joy and hope.
And for this, Madam Commissioner, it is essential that different figures be allocated to the health sector in our cooperation.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, once again, for sad reasons, we are discussing here the situation in Guinea-Bissau, which is truly distressing.
It is a country that has experienced chronic instability for many years, having attempted a path toward democracy in the early 1990s.

Nothing went well; there was a coup d'état, a minor civil war, and the country has lived in great political and military instability, with deep tensions and rivalries, and now, most recently, also the very troubling presence of drug trafficking, increasingly evident to all observers with an interest in the matter.
We strongly condemn these attacks, the bombing death of Chief of Staff Tagme Naouye, and also the particularly barbaric, to say nothing of savage, assassination of President Nino Vieira.
Regardless of your past, we express our solidarity with your families, the people of Guinea-Bissau, and we regret and strongly condemn these attacks.
We wish for a return to normality, and the lesson I draw—and would like to emphasize in this resolution—is that impunity is not the remedy.
In the past, regarding the murder of Assumane Mané and General Alvares M. Ciabra, why were eyes turned away so that the perpetrators were not found and brought to justice?
It is evident that this is not possible.
Consequently, we must make it clear to the State of Guinea-Bissau that those responsible must be identified, the guilty must be brought to justice, and we must provide all necessary assistance.

I would also like, to conclude, to draw attention to the concern we have observed regarding the presence of drug trafficking throughout the region, the risk this poses for the European Union as well, and the striking, undeniable presence in Guinea-Bissau.
I would also like to appeal for closer ties with Cape Verde in this context.

We have established a special partnership with Cape Verde. Cape Verde has very close ties with Guinea, possesses extensive knowledge, but is also highly vulnerable—this is essential for our own European security as well.
Therefore, the intensification of our special partnership with Cape Verde is also very important in this context, thank you very much.
Madam President, colleagues, I would first like to thank everyone for the work accomplished.

The three lines outlined in the Green Paper—concentration, interconnection, and cooperation—can provide solutions to some obstacles hindering the harmonious development of the Union, particularly the negative effects associated with

concentration of economic activity, inequalities in access to markets and services resulting from distance, and divisions imposed both by borders between member states—particularly the less advantaged ones—and also between regions.
We should therefore promote the improvement of synergies among these policies through effective methods for assessing their territorial impact.

For this reason, I have consistently advocated the establishment of supplementary qualitative indicators, with the aim of improving the implementation and execution of corresponding policies on the ground, taking into account the different territorial specificities.
For now, GDP continues to be the sole criterion for eligibility to receive support from the Structural Funds.

However, this definition of supplementary indicators and the conduct of territorial assessments should not lead to additional bureaucracy or delays, but rather to simplification, the implementation of new policies, and supportive actions for territorial cohesion.
The fifth Interim Report, and to conclude, Mr. President, makes a specific reference to the transition regions located between the convergence regions and the competitiveness and employment regions.
It is essential to note that these regions need a clearer status, with greater security and stability for their development. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, in the context of this major debate on territorial cohesion and the future of economic and social cohesion policy, I could not fail to mention the very specific issues facing the outermost regions.

Regions that, due to the combination and persistence of a certain set of geographical factors, reveal a high level of economic and social vulnerability, particularly felt in the context of a severe international crisis such as the one we are currently experiencing.
I would therefore like to appeal to the European Commission, and in particular to Commissioner Hübner, to pay particular attention to the effects of the current crisis on the outermost regions.

An assessment of these effects in each of the outermost regions, particularly in tourism, construction, and rising unemployment, would be important in enabling a specific European response to these regions.

Therefore, I challenge the European Commission to develop a European response to the crisis for the outermost regions, going beyond the measures already announced within the framework of economic and social cohesion policy for the majority of European regions.
a specific European response to the crisis for the outermost regions.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, in response to the global financial crisis, the European economic recovery plan states that cohesion policy significantly contributes to public investment by Member States and regions, and should serve as a means of overcoming the current crisis.
It specifically suggests the adoption of actions in priority areas of the Lisbon Strategy, in order to achieve growth and employment.
All activated instruments aim to achieve this purpose and accelerate the attainment of results.

In this regard, expanding the scope of the European Globalisation Adjustment Fund and accelerating the European Social Fund particularly bear the responsibility of addressing the multiple situations of social and economic emergency requiring support.
I am certain that, today as in the past, your action will be all the more effective the more complementary your actions and domains are.

However, since no new funds or new lines of action on the ground have been foreseen, it is particularly important that the European Social Fund be fully utilized in combating unemployment and the rapidly increasing pressure of competitiveness in the European economy, resulting from the current financial crisis and economic slowdown.

I would like to emphasize that the Committee on Rules has repeatedly identified simplification as essential for improving the management and implementation of structural funds, as the European Commission is now proposing.

Urgency was requested of us in this matter; however, in adopting this package of regulations, we took this into account, and this institution has never shied away from assuming its political responsibilities.

Therefore, despite the many perspectives aroused by the particular circumstances of the moment, in addition to the guarantees of procedural speed and the actual benefit for citizens that the proposal aims to achieve, we refrain at this stage from presenting new proposals or amendments.
However, we emphasize the need to immediately initiate a process of evaluating this fund, with a view to carrying out a complementary review as soon as possible.
The crisis, among other aspects, highlighted the significant imbalances between the countries in the eurozone, perhaps the main source of tension.
conclusion of the report on the Economic and Monetary Union, but above all, the extreme vulnerability of countries that, although belonging to the European Union, do not belong to the eurozone.

Hence, the reinforcement of the resources available at the European level to support balance of payments crises is absolutely indispensable, and hence we welcome the increase from 12 to 25 and then to 50 million euros of necessary funds.
However, the most important thing is to address the root causes of the problems, rather than merely their most apparent manifestations.
Today, the enlarged Europe and the euro area must reinterpret the meaning of European solidarity and the objectives of cohesion and real convergence.
It is necessary to assess, in this light, the capacity of European horizontal policies, but it is also essential to revisit new financing instruments, such as eurobonds.
Financial assistance to the balance of payments is necessary, but it is far from being sufficient to ensure the sustainability of the European project and the single European currency.
Mr. President of the Commission, Mr. President-in-Office, we are facing an unprecedented crisis.

It was indeed not the right moment to discuss internally how we should organize ourselves. Therefore, my wish is that the Treaty of Lisbon be rapidly ratified, so that we may face the global scene united and solidarity, as Europeans.

Regarding the economic crisis, in the report I authored, which was overwhelmingly approved by this Parliament, it was acknowledged that the recovery plan subsequently presented by the Commission was insufficient.
Today we are certain that it is not sufficient.
A crisis that began in the financial sector spreads to the real economy, then to employment, and subsequently to society.
It is insufficient and out of focus.

This is the Commission's opportunity to address the genuine concerns of Europeans through serious financial regulation, rather than through the slightest and most fragmented measures emanating from the European Parliament.

possible, as it is currently underway, not in the form of individual national plans, but of a genuine European initiative, not through scattered means, but by focusing on the real challenge I am addressing.

In every crisis lies an opportunity; this is the opportunity for the Commission and Europe to address the genuine concerns of European citizens, and I hope this will be achieved through a new approach to the recovery plan.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I would like to express, not only on behalf of the PPE, as has already been done by many members, but particularly on behalf of the Portuguese PPE deputies, our great satisfaction with the outcome of the referendum in Ireland.
We believe this clearly strengthens the prospects for the development and evolution of the European Union, creating the conditions for a new phase.

We naturally wish to congratulate all those who contributed to the Treaty of Lisbon, in particular the Commission and the Vice-President present here, as well as, naturally, the Portuguese Presidency of the European Union, which

Here is a very important document, and thus we would also like to express our gratitude by noting that, despite all these challenges and still awaiting the position of the Czech Republic, the Portuguese will joyfully see their capital's name associated.

It is a decisive step in the evolution of the European Union, and I would like to say that, from this perspective, we may also count on another flag, one that will always be championed by Portuguese members of the PPE, namely the flag of national parliaments.
We argue that the Treaty of Lisbon takes a decisive step toward incorporating the presence of national parliaments into European democracy.

And therefore we believe that this dimension, which is the most important aspect of democracy, namely the representative dimension, and not, as we sometimes hear in this Chamber, the referendary dimension.

If the fundamental and essential aspect of democracy is representation rather than direct voting, then the Treaty of Lisbon also represents a significant step toward enhancing the participation of national representative democracies in the European process.
Therefore, we are very pleased with this decision by the Irish people.

Mr. President, Madam Minister, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, a brief intervention to welcome the historic and broad outcome of the referendum in Ireland and to highlight the decisive role played by Fine Gael and its leader, and incoming Prime Minister of Ireland, Enda Kenny, in taking charge of this process.
A process in which it became clearly evident the need to bring the European Union much closer to its citizens.
They are, we are all of us, the primary recipients of the Union's actions.
It is essential that everyone is aware of the impact and benefits that Europe has in our lives on a daily basis.
Europe is our living space.
What is good for Europe is good for each Member State, and good for its citizens.
It is undoubtedly the case with the Treaty of Lisbon.
With this treaty, it becomes much clearer that one can simultaneously be a proud and committed national citizen and a staunch defender of the European project.

And the European Union, having concluded a debate too long on its organization and functioning, will be able to focus much more effectively—and with far more powerful tools—on the real challenges facing Europeans: competitiveness, growth, and employment.
I conclude, Mr. President, Madam Minister, with a proposal intended for future generations.

Given the lack of information regarding the values, competences, objectives, and modes of operation of the European Union, the European Parliament should propose, at the end of compulsory education in each Member State, a new compulsory subject in European studies.
In an objective, truthful, and concrete manner, European youth will truly understand our principles, who we are, what we do, and where we want to go.

It is through knowledge, acquired from a young age, that they will come to understand throughout life the potential and immense value of Europe's identity—this unique project of voluntary sharing of sovereignty on a continental scale.

Mr. President, Mr. President of the Commission, I begin by congratulating you on this first debate, on what this new instrument of political oversight represents for the advancement and development of parliamentary democracy in Europe.
It is the European citizens, through their representatives, who stand to gain.

Taking into account the most recent developments—the Irish referendum, Poland's ratification, and the latest statements by the President of the Czech Republic—I would like to ask for your assessment of the process of entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon.
Efforts have been made by the Chairperson of the Commission.
and what is your forecast regarding a possible entry into force date for the treaty, and on the other hand, whether at this moment and based on that forecast, the Commission

Some transitional measures have already been taken regarding the shift from the Treaty of Nice to the Treaty of Lisbon, or we are still in a phase of, say, anticipation, therefore, a phase of waiting to see.
Mr. President of the Commission, hearing this, I would like to know the following at this stage, when everyone is awaiting the formation of the Commission—what is your vision, Mr. President?
Will we now, up to the moment of final ratification by the Czech Republic, have a commission in office, or will they proceed with the appointment procedures for the new commissioners?

Mr. President, it is essential that the primary objective of establishing the European External Action Service materialize the efforts of the European institutions to ensure the creation of an effective service, capable of coordinating, formulating, and implementing a European response to the international challenges of today.
It should also represent more than the sum of its parts; it should be an added value compared to the current efforts of the Member States and the European Union.
In this sense, it seems essential to me that what defines the specificity of the Union should be at the heart of this new reality, and that specificity is the community method.
That is why I fully support the Brok report, which advocates for and safeguards a close coordination between the Commission and the future Service.

I wish, in this context, to highlight two aspects: the importance of close coordination between the President of the Commission and the High Representative, also Vice-President of the Commission, to ensure effectiveness and proper functioning.
Lastly, the need for an alliance between the Parliament and the Commission to combat the impending intergovernmental drift that could undermine the effective operation of the service.
The Parliament must therefore remain vigilant and ensure that the European External Action Service becomes a center of excellence representative of the best expertise in foreign policy.
Mr. President, Commissioner, first of all, I wish to emphasize the importance of this issue raised by Deputy Gloriano, as it is a matter of the most fundamental justice.

We all fully understand that, in a situation of an H1N1 pandemic, it will be the countries in the south that will suffer the most from this problem and from the consequences of the virus's spread, and not the countries in the north.

And every year we passively witness the death of millions of citizens due to pandemics that have already been eradicated or are treatable in Northern countries. An extensive list of malaria, dengue, yellow fever, tuberculosis,
We have no limits for this list, and we passively witness this problem.

We effectively use countries in the Global South as sources of free samples of new virus strains, enabling us to improve treatments in Northern countries; however, despite all this, we remain without an effective response to these problems, and thus, to the questions that have been raised, I would simply like to add a few more.
We genuinely want to address this issue as a public health problem or as a business.
Because if it is a public health issue, we must ensure equal access to treatment in all regions of the world, and for this, efforts need to be more effective.
On the other hand, I would like to ask—and I hope this is not the case—whether we are expecting, after all, that vaccine stocks in Europe become so overwhelmed.
that secondary IFPs turn out so negative in this scenario, only then would we have an act of generosity, and suddenly begin sending the necessary vaccines to countries in the Global South.

Lastly, I would simply like to ask whether the European Union, we Europeans, and the Commission feel comfortable with a system or model in which northern countries address pandemics through a preventive approach, while southern countries deal with them only through palliative measures or what remains afterward.

Mr. President, first of all, I would like to express my full support regarding the issue raised by Member Reul, and particularly to highlight the underlying, fundamental political issue at stake in this matter, which precisely concerns the joint programming of research.

What occurred regarding joint programming on neurodegenerative diseases, and in particular Alzheimer's disease, was that, during the process, the rules changed: we no longer had a report, but instead a resolution, thereby eliminating our capacity for co-decision-making on this issue, and we ceased to be members of parliament, becoming consultants instead.
And therefore, I would like to know whether, at the very least, what we recommend will be taken into account or not.
The programming of research activities, in whatever field, is a political choice, not a technical one, and in this sense, the role of Parliament should be recognized and strengthened.
It seems to me that defining priorities that are political means that these priorities must be subject to scrutiny, must be transparent, and must be democratic.
What occurred with the process regarding the joint decision and joint programming of research on Alzheimer's disease is a situation that must not be allowed to recur, and if it does,

Please repeat that they should inform us in advance. I therefore hope that the last thing that could happen to us is that the decisions and recommendations presented by Parliament on this matter are not taken into account.
Mr. President, last month the regions of Europe were particularly in the spotlight.
The European Week dedicated to regions and cities highlighted the role of regions as a fundamental component of the European response to global challenges.
Thus they concluded, leaving no room for doubt, President Durão Barroso, Commissioner Samezky, and the hundreds of debates that took place.

Moreover, at a more specific level, the holding of the important meeting of the Conference of Presidents of the European Ultraperipheral Regions, which for the first time included participation by members of the European Parliament, marks a milestone in regional policy in support of European integration.
Global challenges and European responses present a great opportunity for a new strategy for the LUP.

These regions, such as the Azores, despite the persistent constraints that everyone is accustomed to hearing about, possess a set of potentialities that offer clear opportunities to advance our progress and provide Europe with a distinct advantage in new, essential domains for addressing global challenges.

It is therefore required that the President of the Commission clearly put an end to the palace coup that the Commission's No Paper indicates was being prepared, while we, as Members of Parliament and European citizens, were advocating for the ratification of the Treaty of Lisbon and its importance in safeguarding territorial cohesion.

First of all, I would like to greet the rapporteurs, particularly Mr. Martin and also Mr. Corbet, and say that, as a member of the PPE, I essentially identify with the amendments to the rules of procedure proposed.
It is essential to emphasize the point that I consider important here and that has not yet been raised tonight, namely the issue of national parliaments.
The democratic strengthening of the European Parliament undoubtedly stems from this regime and the Treaty of Lisbon, but European democracy also arises from its connection to national parliaments.
And what this Regulation or proposal now aims to do is precisely to establish these two democratic legitimacies, strengthening democracy.

On the one hand, democracy resulting from the European Parliament's own instruments, from its instruments of oversight regarding its legislative capacity; on the other hand, democratic cooperation by national parliaments through various instruments.
And that is why I am, so to speak, fully in tune with the proposals made.
I do not agree with colleague Duff when he believes we should postpone the termination of mechanisms linking us to national parliaments.
I believe we can do this immediately, in light of the Treaty of Lisbon, and that this is very important.
I would also like to say that I support the proposal put forward by my colleagues Sayer and Brock regarding representation in the COSAC, and finally, that I am
Fully aligned with the observations made here by colleague Brok and other colleagues regarding the primacy of the Parliament and its members over the Parliament's services.
Mr. President, Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I congratulate the rapporteur colleague for the work carried out in this report and for her intervention a short while ago.

This new microfinance instrument will enable the granting of microcredit to small businesses and to individuals who have lost their jobs and wish to create their own business and their own employment opportunity.
This is particularly important at a time when an economic crisis is expected to result in the loss of three and a half million jobs within the European Union alone.

As a consequence of the economic slowdown, banks have ceased granting loans for job creation and business development, and access to credit has become more difficult at a time when it should be more accessible.

Now, this new microfinance mechanism will counteract the current trend of restricted access to credit, facilitating the acquisition of funds necessary for launching new businesses and creating new jobs.
The European Commission's proposal aims to reallocate 100 million euros from the PROGRESS programme's budget to this financing instrument.
We cannot agree with this proposal.
The financial and economic crisis is equally a social crisis.
Thus, the reallocation of resources from PROGRESS, which targets the most vulnerable groups, is not entirely the most appropriate solution.
For this reason, support is given for the creation of a separate budgetary heading to finance this instrument, as well as an increase in funding to 150 million euros.

We also agree with the need to clarify in the legislation itself that the target group consists of vulnerable groups who face difficulties in entering or re-entering the labor market and who are at risk of social exclusion.
Thus, reference to specific groups should be omitted.
Finally, we emphasize that it is essential for individuals receiving financial support to also have adequate training. Thank you very much.

Thank you very much, Mr. President. I would like to begin by congratulating the Swedish Presidency and, in particular, Prime Minister Reinfeldt, both on behalf of the PPE and on behalf of the Portuguese delegation of the PPE.
Our assessment of the Swedish Presidency is one of near-total success, essentially in four key areas.

The first, in the institutional sphere, the contribution of the Swedish Presidency to the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, and to the highly successful election of the President of the Commission.
in particular, all matters relating to ratification, was extremely professional and met the standard expected of best practices within the European Union.
Naturally, for a country like Portugal, which played a decisive role in the approval of the Treaty of Lisbon, this contribution by the Swedish Presidency is invaluable.
A second point is the climate agenda, where, naturally, the Commission's work was also extremely important.
We should say that, from my perspective and that of many PPE deputies, it is in the area of climate change that the European Union has achieved its greatest success.
It is truly confronting the front line of the global fight, and this is due to the efforts of both the Swedish Presidency and, in particular, the Commission's Presidency.
We also consider this assessment very positive.
A third point is that of financial regulation, which, especially with this latest Council, has taken a step we consider decisive and which could have a major impact on overcoming the crisis.
And therefore, I would also like to congratulate on the achievement of an agreement in this area.
And finally, I would like to say a few words about an area that personally means a great deal to me, namely the Stockholm Programme, and consequently the area of security, justice, and freedom.

I believe that, having followed both the tempering process and especially the process of the AIA, I consider this Stockholm programme absolutely essential, and I would like to congratulate the Swedish Presidency and Prime Minister Reinfeldt on this.

Mr. President, for the recognition of professional qualifications, as is well known, 15 directives have been consolidated into a single legislative act, which has led to various practical problems in implementation across the different Member States.

The difficulties are well known, and the Commission should inventory the critical points and conduct an analysis of the problematic areas arising in the application of this key instrument of the internal market.
The protectionism manifested at various levels constitutes an obstacle not only to the free movement of people but also to the internal market.
We must strive to identify the ambiguous aspects so that the directive's effectiveness is not undermined by suspicions.
The Union is not asked to do what member states should do themselves, but there is a need for an effort to facilitate, which helps dismantle professional corporatism.
An improvement in the application of this directive should involve the dissemination of qualification recognition through the SOLVIT network and the EURES network by encouraging
common platforms, implementation of effective contact points, and, no less importantly, should provide coordination between this directive and the Services Directive.

Mr. President, Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen Members of Parliament, it is urgent that we initiate a debate on the community budget and on how, in the future, we can contribute to its sustainability and transparency.

The post-2013 financial framework should be built on solidarity and territorial cohesion, values crucial for regions such as Madeira, which face persistent challenges and therefore require sustained support.
I would like to share with you some ideas that I believe should always be present when reflecting on and shaping cohesion policy.

The Commission has, on several occasions, highlighted certain aspects that I consider essential, such as the need for greater flexibility, simplicity, efficiency, and a stronger focus on results in the design and implementation of this policy.
However, there are issues that raise some concern, particularly when considering the needs of the outermost regions.
In this matter, allow me three brief remarks. First, the current approach based on regions should prevail over alternative guidelines, such as sectors of added value.

Such a reorientation could undermine Objective 2, which currently benefits about two-thirds of European regions, and may potentially be counterproductive in regions with distinct economic profiles and competitive advantages. Secondly, it seems to me that maintaining the proximity criterion in the cohesion policy is vital.

it should continue to prioritize the allocation and management of structural funds from a regional perspective, rather than a national one, or even a centralized approach at the European level. Third and finally, but no less importantly, it is worth recalling and emphasizing Article 349.
Article 349 of the Lisbon Treaty provides special treatment for the outermost regions regarding access to structural funds, precisely due to their economic and social situation.
The situation is exacerbated by enduring conditions and unique characteristics that give rise to these persistent difficulties, thereby necessitating the requested permanent assistance.
Mr. President, in light of the urgency, what we witnessed in Copenhagen were several steps backward.

It is true that Copenhagen brought together more heads of state than Kyoto, but it also created greater divisions, and as a result, we ended up with a situation of each state for itself, where each country can set its own goals and, moreover, within a voluntary regime.
It should be noted that if we have a target of two degrees Celsius or funding targets without any clarification, they also cannot serve as consolation.
And therefore, what I believe is that we must act in terms of the future, and acting in terms of the future means moving beyond rhetoric to action.
The European Union has always wanted to be a leader, and has always stated as much.
But when we arrived in Copanha, he ended up hesitating, failing to define or defend the goals he had previously stated as his own, which he would not compromise on.

And therefore, I ask whether, for the future, we will not be more just, whether we will continue to allow the poorest to bear the heaviest burden, at the expense of this failure and of decisions made by some over others.
Mr. President, I would like to draw attention here to what is currently happening in Portugal, where it is evident that the media is one of the principal problems in the world.
It brought to light a government plan to control newspapers, television stations, and radio stations, which undermines freedom of expression.
This week as well, a well-known journalist, Mario Crespo, had one of his columns censored, also due to a suggestion or apparent suggestion from the prime minister.
In light of this, Prime Minister José Sócrates must provide substantial explanations to the nation, must clarify that he is not undermining, adjusting, or censoring freedom of expression in Portugal.

As we are currently proceeding, Portugal is no longer a State of Law, but a formal State of Law, in which the prime minister confines himself to mere formalities, procedures, and formalism, and refuses to provide substantial explanations.
For Portugal, we want a material rule of law.

Mr. President, the Green Paper on the reform of the common fisheries policy, whose rapporteur is my colleague from the General Affairs Committee, Maria do Céu Patrão Neves, presents a unique opportunity for debate on a sector of great importance in balancing the local development of the overseas regions.

Madara follows the ongoing discussion with particular interest and considers it essential to establish a distinction between artisanal and industrial fleets, which should be subject to different fishing regimes.
It is essential to uphold a principle of positive discrimination by restricting access to territorial waters within the exclusive economic zone to local artisanal fleets only.
On wood, priority is given to supporting the modernization of vessels, as well as management practices that ensure the long-term conservation of a broad range of species.
Only in this way will we be able to avoid unsustainable situations for economic, environmental, and social balance.
Lastly, we will strive for fisheries in the outermost regions to have permanent, not time-limited, support.
This report on the FEADER aims to extend the eligibility of interventions in the housing sector to benefit marginalized communities in our Member States.
To date, this support applied only to marginalized communities residing in the urban areas of the new member states.
That is, since the vast majority of this type of community lives in rural areas and shelters, these communities could not benefit from support aimed at replacing substandard housing.
Ultimately, it is about how to undermine a vaccine and grant greater possibilities to member states and their regions in addressing the specific challenges faced by these communities.
I also welcome the equal treatment of all member states, as marginalized communities in need of support and integration deserve our concern, regardless of the member state to which they belong.

By adopting this important report in first reading in the fight against poverty and for human dignity, its swift implementation within the framework of the economic recovery plan is also achieved, providing a prompt response to the crisis affecting us.

Mr. President, Commissioner, allow me to begin by highlighting the role of the rapporteur, Mr. Van Nistelrooy, for his work in seeking consensus on this matter, as well as for the willingness he has consistently shown to incorporate other contributions into his report.
Tomorrow we will vote on amendments to the FEDER regulation that assume particular importance for the so-called older Member States and also for Portugal.
These changes will allow extending the use of the FEDER in the housing sector in favor of marginalized communities, as currently this fund can only be allocated to urban development operations.

The amendments he proposed, together with other colleagues and which were approved by a large majority in the Committee on Regional Development, will enable the former Member States, and not just the new ones as originally envisaged, to also benefit from this financing opportunity.

With this, I sought to prevent the establishment of a precedent that I consider dangerous and that would exclude all the former member states from this and, likely, from other additional uses of community assistance.
This would make no sense, since housing-related issues, particularly among marginalized communities, exist both in the new and the old member states.

I take this opportunity to reiterate that seniority of accession to the European Union should not be a criterion for the allocation of structural funds, and that this criterion should be abandoned outright.

The negotiation of the post-2013 cohesion policy should be based on solidarity, aiming at territorial cohesion, and should be oriented toward rewarding, rather than penalizing, regions that have demonstrated exemplary use of community funding.

Mr. President, at this crucial moment, I would like to essentially emphasize to the Commission and to the President of the Commission the way in which they have worked with this Parliament throughout these months of tenure.
There is no body in the world with executive and legislative initiative powers that is subject to its president presenting a program before Parliament, followed by hearings with all the groups.

deputies to a vote by an absolute and decisive majority, to bring here all the commissioners to be heard one by one, each with their own program, and three to four hours of direct questioning, with questions and answers.
someone who agreed to negotiate with Parliament a framework agreement, in which they agreed, first and foremost, to justify and expand the Parliament's powers in the field of legislative initiative.

who accepted the principle of full access to information, whether in legislative and political action or in international negotiations, who accepted taking into account the opinion of Parliament regarding the commissioners and the reshuffling of the commissioners.
This means, once and for all and from my standpoint, that the Commission has demonstrated, from July until now, its willingness to maintain a close relationship with Parliament and that it is

The President did so, clearly, vividly, and unambiguously demonstrating that the strategic alliance established by the Treaty of Lisbon for the community method referred to by colleague Sarkozy Zwolowski is the alliance between the Parliament and the Commission.
And therefore, the Commission and the framework agreement deserve the full support of the PPE.
Mr. President, five years after the Orange Revolution, the Ukrainians seem to view this period as a missed opportunity.
And the blame is not only on your political elites.

that many reforms remained unfulfilled, many never went beyond paper.

and translate the unworthy and commendable intentions inherent in their genesis.
