Without a free and independent justice system, there is no rule of law, human rights are not protected, there is no foreign investment, and there is no progress.
But the blame for this disillusionment also lies heavily within this Parliament and, fundamentally, in many Member States.
I would like you, for a few seconds, to imagine yourself as a citizen of a European country that, due to the twists of history, is still not a member of the European Union.
What is spiritism that is not?
solidarity.

Yet many chancelleries, solely concerned with not offending or displeasing Moscow, exploited and abused the ongoing internal conflicts in Kyiv to avoid unequivocally stating that Ukraine is an independent and sovereign country.
Thus, if that is the freely expressed will of its people and provided they meet the established criteria, Ukraine may aspire to become, in the future, a member of the European Union.
I would like to emphasize that in island regions, air transport provides the only means of access, and operating conditions are determined by the scale of their passenger and cargo flows.

There are insular regions where the abundance of these flows is attractive to many airlines, fostering competition and low transport prices, thus improving accessibility; others, such as the Azores, have comparatively limited flows, making them less appealing to airline operators.

As a result, airfares are higher, acting as a barrier to population mobility and a limiting factor in attracting tourists, which hinders the flourishing and development of this vast potential for economic diversification.
This situation is particularly detrimental during times of crisis such as the one we are experiencing.

Therefore, temporary support is essential to stimulate increased flows, enabling the market to function properly, while also aligning these regions with high tourism potential with the objectives of the trans-European networks in bringing Europe's peripheries closer together.
Mr. President, I rise today in this Chamber with a voice choked by the dismay of one who has lived through and personally witnessed the tragedy that struck Madeira last Saturday.

and we fear that the number of victims may increase.

The economic and social impact of this catastrophe prevents people from resuming a normal life course.
You now need to look ahead.
I also need to convey a message of solidarity, hope, and trust to people.

It is necessary to provide assistance, and it is in this chapter that the European Union and, in particular, the Parliament, have a role to play. They are essential in the process of mobilizing the Solidarity Fund.

I appeal here to the Commission and its President, Mr. Barroso, particularly to Commissioner for Regional Development João H. Ahn, and I welcome your initiative to visit Madeira at the earliest opportunity.
Requesting that he also carry this message of hope, assistance, and support for reconstruction, because now is the time to rebuild what nature, unfortunately and once again, has taken from me.
But I am certain that we will do it, because, as the anthem of Madeira subtly suggested, the Madeiran people are humble, stoic, and brave.
It is the people who, amidst the rocks, toiled the land, heroes of labor in the wild mountain.
Through wood, he will honor his history, and at the end of his work, he will struggle and attain his well-being and glory.
We begin today's plenary session with a minute of silence.

And it was a minute of silence with two meanings: one of solidarity with the victims of this catastrophe that has devastated Madeira and their families, and another of tribute to all workers who have tirelessly assisted these victims.
But the moment of silence should make us reflect, and should also guide us toward a reflection that goes beyond the one we are here undertaking.
What we hear in the wood is a phenomenon that will tend to recur increasingly.
Climate change causes extreme events to recur, and we must have responses ready for them.
We cannot prevent natural disasters, but we should not be condemned to their always having such dramatic consequences for people's lives.
That is why we must strive for planning and zoning policies that are oriented toward the public good.

But I would like to conclude, Mr. President, with what is most important and what has brought us here: that the European Commission, the European Parliament, and the European institutions must have the capacity to provide an urgent response to an emergency situation, and for this, the necessary financial resources and the required speed are essential.
a gesture of solidarity in honor of the families and friends of the victims of the tragic events on Saturday on the island of Madeira.

Mr. President, I too wish to express my solidarity with the people of Madeira and to urgently appeal to the European institutions, and in particular the Commission, to unlock all available resources, all means at their disposal, in order to provide this assistance.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, and Mr. Council, we are here discussing the reinvention of the energy system at the European scale through investment in low-carbon technologies.
I believe it is one of the very important challenges, as indeed referred to in the communication being made.
It is one of the challenges we must address centrally, but there is no clarification here, which is why it is important to raise these questions and request clarification.
It seems that, besides the lack of clarification regarding funding, there is also a lack of political clarification.
Efficiency in energy use, carbon capture, nuclear power, biofuels, solar energy are discussed, and efficiency in energy use is even mentioned as the most cost-effective way to reduce emissions.
It surprises me that there are many other far cheaper ways to reduce carbon emissions that do not even involve this kind of communication, such as, for instance, right away, reducing consumption.
energetic, microgeneration, distribution, and different forms of distribution and different projects that do not have to go solely through large structures and access to energy.
I hope that there we were certainly contributing to greater financial clarity to make the plan cheaper.

And therefore, within this political clarification and within this financial clarification, there remains still the need to address what appears to me to be an absolutely crucial point: the constant emphasis placed on public-private partnerships, as if they were the solution to everything.
When asked where the money comes from, the Commission tells us it comes from where it is.
I confess that I am not any clearer when told the money will come from where it is, without being told exactly where that is.

And therefore, this reliance on public or private partnerships—systematically emphasized when discussing low-carbon technologies—surely comes with one certainty: knowing where the money does not come from, and knowing that these are public or private partnerships, we can determine who will ultimately pay.
It is the taxpayers who pay initially, and it is the consumers—who happen to also be taxpayers in the end.
And we know that those who now pay less are private institutions and organizations, and that those making investments are compensated by it, yet ultimately end up monopolizing the profits.
Without this clarification, it seems to me that, yet again, we are systematically passing on to future generations the bill for the transition of the European energy model.

Mr. President, the European Union recently concluded, within the framework of the World Trade Organization, an agreement with countries in Latin America, in which it committed to substantially and progressively reducing tariffs on imports of bananas from those countries.

I draw your attention to the fact that such an agreement has directly negative effects on European banana producers, particularly on banana producers from Madeira in Portugal, but even more severely on the producers from the Canary Islands in Spain.

I recall that the situation of producers in these regions is already seriously hampered by the physical and orographic characteristics of the islands and by the size of their holdings, which forces them to face persistent difficulties that hinder their development.

It is therefore urgent to take measures to protect the producers of bananas, timber, and the Canary Islands, given the serious and immediate impact that the new Geneva Agreement is having on the European Union's import market, and consequently on the production and marketing of bananas from these outermost regions.

Mr. President, Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, we are facing an acceleration of a global transformation that has devastating consequences for our economic, political, and social systems, and consequently for all our citizens.
Today we witness an unprecedented weakening of states' capacity to respond.
The European Union consequently needs to identify common and allied causes and act clearly and unitedly on the world stage.
Extraordinary situations require clarity in joint action.

If we do not take the rigorous and collective accountability measures that are required, Europe will be condemned to marginalization and impoverishment, and only a strong Europe, respectful of its common rules, will be able to adequately respond to the new times.
Weak and heavily indebted states lack the capacity to protect their citizens.

Consequently, we need to be able to regain citizens' trust, win the battles over stability, budgetary discipline, job creation, the stability of the monetary union, globalization, and strategic choices.
This, under penalty of compromising our future.
And one wins by betting on the future through social cohesion, ensuring peace, and building a new model based on the values of freedom, social justice, and responsibility.
Thus, the 2020 Strategy and the Integrated Guidelines for Europe 2020 constitute essential tools for a new cycle of growth and employment in Europe.
For proper implementation and execution, it is necessary to establish clear and measurable objectives regarding employment, education, and poverty reduction.

It is also decisive for the success of the strategy that everything possible be done to facilitate the transposition by Member States of their national objectives and to ensure the proper implementation of this strategy. Thank you very much.
When the economic and monetary union completed ten years, the Commission regarded the euro as the Union's most successful project.
Today we would have difficulty asserting this without limitations, because the crisis has demonstrated the evidence that the economic and monetary union is an unfinished project.
Minimal rescue measures were taken in an emergency context, but let us not deceive ourselves regarding the pressure conditions and the direct interests of the main parties.
The member countries, the painful nature of the decisions, and their slowness have weakened the image of the euro and the European Union in the eyes of citizens and the world.
We must face the facts.
The European Union and the euro are a process of integration, not merely cooperation among countries.
They are an unfinished project that, at this moment, either strengthens or faces serious risks of disappearing.
There are three issues that must be addressed first. It is important to consolidate the instruments that have been launched in this state of emergency; it is necessary to establish a monetary fund.

The European euro requires progress toward Eurobonds, progress toward the European supervisory and regulatory architecture, and progress toward a European dimension in the management of banking crises.
The second dimension is to recognize that there is no strong currency if the economy is weak; the projected 1% growth for the European Union is unsustainable in terms of income.
both in terms of its demographic composition and in combating unemployment. The 2020 Strategy is nothing more than a set of good ideas without the means to implement them.

Third dimension, Commissioner Lehne, fiscal consolidation is important, but it cannot coexist with growing divergences among the Member States comprising the Union. The convergence policy must be urgently reviewed. Thank you very much, Mr. President.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. Several of my colleagues from the PPE have already provided a very comprehensive overview of the situation.

and the conditions necessary for the single currency to be strengthened and for this project of monetary and economic integration to move forward.

Apology for the necessary measures to ensure the euro emerges strengthened, and to enable the realization of this space of integration and this dream— a dream of the PPE and a dream of us all.

I would like to begin by saying that, in our opinion, the report on the assessment of the results of the 2006 roadmap for gender equality, which aims to influence future strategies on this issue, could have been more decisive and less controversial.

In fact, the approach taken toward several ethically sensitive issues—particularly the issue of women's sexual and reproductive health—should have been different if the genuine objective had been to achieve the broadest possible consensus.

That said, I would like to emphasize that during periods of normal economic growth, concern regarding women's rights—particularly the disparities between men and women in the workplace, society, and the family—is significant; however, these problems are profoundly exacerbated by economic and social crises such as the one we are currently experiencing.
Hence the need for a strengthened strategic commitment by the Member States, at the level of local and regional authorities and community institutions, regarding the protection of human rights.

We must state that, in line with what President Daul and colleague Werner Langen said, it is essential that there be economic governance within the Union, but that this economic governance should be centered on the Commission.

Also among those in the 2020 Strategy, I would particularly highlight the issues of education and training for young people, as well as innovation, research, and development.

Because these are the niches that will enable Europe to remain competitive in the global arena. And today I must say, unfortunately, that the European educational model has failed, and has failed when compared to the models of emerging countries.

Like India, like China, like Brazil, it is today a less demanding and less rigorous model, and thus it is also requested that, in the boldness of this idea of innovation, development, research, and education, there be rigor and high standards within the context of European educational systems.
Only with this rigor and high standards will we prepare our youth to compete in the global market.

We cannot focus solely on this year's crisis or the next two or three years; we must think strategically about Europe's future over the next ten to twenty years, and that depends on an education system that is rigorous and demanding.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, never before has the European project, built with such effort over the past 50 years, been so threatened.

The frustration of European citizens is immense—the financial system, which was meant to serve them, has destroyed their savings, their pensions, their investment plans, their jobs, in short, their future.
Regarding the necessary strengthening of the entire financial services market—banks, insurance, and securities—the path to follow is clear and lies in the architecture we are discussing here today.
We actually need a strong European supervisory architecture, covering all products and market participants, equipped with methodologies and instruments
effective intervention and one capable of responding to a reality of a market that, particularly in the European context, does not recognize national boundaries.

Risk cannot and should not be eliminated from the financial market, but we do need regulation that makes this risk more transparent and prevents the accumulation of bubbles whose bursting would affect the entire system and spill over into the real economy.

However, if new crises occur in the future despite these safeguards, it is crucial to have mechanisms in place that limit contagion, particularly with regard to the banking system, as it is essential for the functioning of the economy and the safeguarding of citizens' savings.
Here, precisely, lies the power and competence of the banking authority in executing a new strategy for managing cross-border banking crises, the subject addressed in my report.

It is not within our power to prevent bank failures, but rather to ensure that their restructuring or eventual liquidation is carried out in a manner that safeguards the stability of the financial system and protects the interests of depositors and other stakeholders.
ordered form, limiting collateral effects on the rest of the system and, above all, preventing taxpayers from bearing the costs of the crises they generate.

To achieve this, a set of new devices is required. It is necessary to adopt common rules, definitions, methodologies, tools, and terminology among all parties involved in restructuring, reorganization, or liquidation operations.
It is necessary to harmonize the relevant national legal frameworks for crisis management, particularly bankruptcy and liquidation laws.
It is necessary to grant supervisors broader powers of intervention.
It is necessary to establish clear criteria, not only quantitative but also qualitative, that enable interventions to be initiated in an institution facing difficulties.
Contingency plans need to be prepared.
This entire process requires time for 27 member states.
Moreover, approximately 50 banks among the 12 operating in Europe account for 70 percent of the assets.
Now, this means an enormous concentration of risk, but also an opportunity to prioritize intervention with regard to them.

There are, therefore, conditions justifying the proposal of a special and urgent process for the coordination of banks, which, due to their cross-border nature and relationships with other banks, may have a systemic impact on the financial system.
Now, for these banks, it is possible to anticipate a special supervision by the new European banking authority, although operating within the College of National Supervisors.
It is possible and urgent that they, in light of the risks they pose, begin contributing to a fund that finances potential interventions.
that are necessary, and it is important that the banking authority establish a specialized unit to coordinate interventions in the event of difficulties at these banks.
Together, the proposed elements ensure that interventions in systemically significant transnational banks are consistent and coordinated among the
different countries, and above all, that a principle so cherished in Europe is upheld—that the costs of pollution caused by these banks be borne by the polluter themselves.
This proposal fits perfectly within what we are discussing, particularly regarding the competences of the banking authority. I would even say it is the most obvious test of its effectiveness.
In the case of the vote in the Economic and Monetary Committee, there was a very large majority in support of this proposal, with 87 votes.
We also count here in the plenary with a strong vote from this institution in support of this proposal, which is visionary, realistic, yet pragmatic.
Let us take an action that is urgent and necessary to rekindle Europeans' hope in Europe, in its financial system, and in particular in the banking system. Thank you very much.

Deforestation, as we know, is one of the planet's main environmental and social problems, and each year we lose 13 million hectares of forest, thus contributing to 20% of global carbon emissions and posing a major threat to biodiversity.

The contribution of deforestation to climate change is undeniable, and thus it is highly responsible for the occurrence of extreme weather events, floods, and even the desertification of vast areas of the planet.
Forest loss puts all of humanity at risk, but this risk and its impact are not shared equally among us.
Communities with fewer resources and those that depend most on forests and nature for survival are the most affected.
Deforestation concerns our coexistence, the scarcity of natural resources, their distribution, the rights of local and indigenous communities, and social justice.
The main cause of deforestation is illegal logging, which accounts for 20 to 40 percent of global industrial wood production.
Therefore, we are also talking about a transfer of wealth from producer countries to consumer countries in this same manner.
This illegal practice jeopardizes the sustainable exploitation of wood and the companies that engage in it.
Words alone are not enough.
This Parliament must adopt strong and decisive measures in this fight.
This report takes a very significant step in this direction by establishing a ban on the trade of timber.
Good intentions alone are not enough either.
Effective means are required, particularly sanctions, which in this case could take three forms.

First, fines that effectively eliminate the economic benefit derived from the illegal practice, aggravated by recidivism; second, the confiscation of the timber and related products; and third, the immediate suspension of the authorization to carry out a commercial activity.

And thus it is because those who exploit or commercialize a natural resource should be socially responsible, and it is also so because those who first place wood or wood products into the European Union should be required to exercise due diligence to ensure that the wood has been harvested legally.
That is why all subsequent operators must provide basic information about this wood, namely its origin and its destination.
The public must also have the right—and I conclude here, Mr. President—the right to access information about who complies and who does not comply. Thank you very much.

Madam President, today we are discussing yet another case of human rights violation—the imprisonment of Syrian activist Aytan al Malé, an 80-year-old lawyer, accused of spreading false and exaggerated information that undermines the national sentiments of his country.

In addition to violating numerous international treaties and conventions, the imprisonment of this human rights defender is also contrary to the country's own legislation, which stipulates that military courts should not have jurisdiction over civilians.
The decision taken regarding this man and other Syrian detainees held for similar reasons, the various detentions, the restrictions on freedom of movement, and the arbitrary measures adopted

The measures taken by the Syrian authorities are practices that contradict Syria's significant role throughout the region and do not reflect the efforts that have been made in this country to improve the social situation.
I repeat, today we are discussing yet another case of human rights violation.
It is not possible to separate it from the various discussions we have had here this week.
I highlight two points: the first is the rejection of the death sentence by stoning imposed on Sakineh Hashemi.
Many voices have come together, and some results have already been achieved.

It is not yet time to sing victories, but even less is it time for us to remain silent. Secondly, today we have adopted an important resolution against the abuses imposed by the Sarkozy government on Romanian and Bulgarian citizens.
The defense of human rights knows no borders and is not a struggle that can be waged solely abroad.
Today we provide a good example of how to contain it also within doors.
The unjustified imprisonment of Aitane al Malé unites us all here today.
She is a human rights advocate.
Human rights defenders must be free individuals, and that is precisely what we demand from the Syrian authorities.
We, too, defenders of human rights, will not leave you alone, thank you very much.

Mr. President, the growing trend whereby the safety profile of new medicines can only be fully understood after they have entered the market necessitates a pharmacovigilance system more effective than any previously in place.
We are facing a typical risk management issue, but at the macro and supranational level, we have brought it closer to citizens now.
New medications have played a fundamental role in public health and advances in healthcare over the past decades.
Adverse drug reactions, however, continue to be the fifth leading cause of hospital death.
The proposals presented reinforce the contribution of medicines to modern public health through a community-based pharmacovigilance system, improving coordination and transparency.
Its objective is the monitoring, early detection, communication, and assessment of adverse reactions.
The new medications will have a graphical symbol indicating that they are still under review.

User participation in monitoring is encouraged, national-level effectiveness studies are promoted, new sensitivity to environmental consequences is fostered, and community monitoring institutions are improved.
That is why I congratulate you, Mr. President, on these proposals.

This directive ensures more active involvement of healthcare professionals and patients in identifying difficulties.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, the objective of territorial cohesion, now enshrined in the Treaty of Lisbon, embodies the value of European solidarity and the commitment of Member States and the Union to reduce the disparities existing among various regions.
However, it is not enough to merely express in the text of the treaties the objectives to be achieved.
There are regions within the Union that face permanent constraints that strongly hinder their economic and social development.

It is therefore necessary to implement specific programs that effectively and concretely overcome the lag in these regions by promoting an adaptation of their development model to their potentials and resources, and by contributing to achieving the objectives of the EU 2020 Strategy.

This incentive to these regions should stem not only from the instruments of regional policy, through its structural adjustment mechanisms, such as the Cohesion Fund and the four Structural Funds, but also
Also from a different use of various sectoral policies, which have a significant territorial impact in each region and can therefore have a stimulating effect on their economies.
For the determination of European assistance to these regions, it is certain that we should continue to use GDP as the primary eligibility indicator.

Nevertheless, and taking into account its natural disadvantages, it becomes pertinent to resort to other measurable criteria capable of providing a more accurate picture of its level of development, such as the unemployment rate, population density, or education level.
Only in this way will it be possible to obtain a more comprehensive portrayal of the complex reality of the most disadvantaged regions.

Bear in mind, for example, the case of the outermost regions, whose status is recognized by the new Treaty and which constitute a group of regions within the European Union possessing specific characteristics and deserving of special treatment as well.
The regions of the ultraperiphery, united by their particular characteristics, also differ among themselves in terms of their levels of development.

Likewise, with regard to these, complementary indicators to GDP should be used, capable of providing a more comprehensive description of their specific reality and thus contributing to a more accurate assessment of their level of development. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, Mr. President, Commissioners, dear colleagues, after four consecutive years of decreasing unemployment rates from 2004 to 2008, the current economic crisis has reversed many of the advances achieved.
Unemployment rates in the European Union have experienced a significant increase; for example, in Portugal we unfortunately reached a record high unemployment rate of 11%.
Another negative phenomenon is youth unemployment in the European Union.
Europe is thus going through a period of transformation and faces enormous challenges.
The crisis erased years of economic and social progress and exposed the vulnerabilities of its member states' economies, and consequently, of the European Union itself.
The 2020 Strategy outlines the paths to follow.
The European Union should intensify all its efforts towards promoting employment and social inclusion, as an integral part of its strategy to combat the economic and social crisis.
Thus, the resolution proposal on the future of the Social Fund currently under discussion is welcomed, as this Fund plays a crucial role in achieving the objectives of the 2020 Strategy.

In fact, this fund is the main financial instrument that enables the European Union to achieve the strategic objectives of its employment policy, by improving the levels of education and qualifications of its citizens.
The ESF should be strengthened as the primary driver of the 2020 Strategy and should be used by Member States to invest in job-related qualifications, training, and professional requalification.

Finally, I would like to emphasize that the reform of the European Social Fund, to be carried out jointly with the reform of the other Structural Funds, should facilitate and simplify its management and implementation.

This is largely due to the forward-looking planning strategy employed for employment, which identifies key factors to act upon, how to achieve the desired objective, and also the use of the European Development Fund as an operational instrument for this strategy, acting through education, training, and employment promotion.

The special situation of the outermost regions must not be forgotten.
This group of regions faces very significant challenges and must continue to benefit in the future from specific provisions in the implementation of European policies.
GDP should continue to be the primary criterion for eligibility, but we must also take into account other indicators that could provide a more comprehensive picture of regional reality.

Industrial accidents can and must be prevented; it is precisely for this purpose that European legislation exists, and it seems obvious that the law was violated.

Mr. President, the price of food has reached its all-time high and this is the seventh consecutive increase.
There are several factors that may explain this increase, the first of which is a short-term factor.
It is related to the coincidence of poor weather conditions in certain countries, such as Russia and Argentina.
But there are two factors that are structuring, of a structural nature, and that is precisely why I bring these two factors forward here.
The first relates to the fact that some food producers and consumers who were exporters have become importers, as is the case with China.
The second relates to the fact that the weight of certain major multinationals enables them to have prices ruled in their favor.
And the example of cereals is this very clear level, dominated by six major multinationals.
This situation thus places farmers in significant uncertainty regarding food product prices.
Europe must therefore resume doing what it has neglected for some time, namely, protecting its producers and consumers.
In a world where everyone resorts to protective measures and multinational corporations manipulate prices, we can no longer afford to be naive. Thank you very much.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, I begin by congratulating my colleague Schwab for the commercial perseverance that led to this important agreement, and I also congratulate all those who collaborated with him on this report.

We aim not only to ensure a high level of protection for European consumers, but also that European businesses, regardless of their size, can supply goods and services to consumers across the 27 Member States without unnecessary legal barriers.

This new directive will put an end to the legislative fragmentation that has been hindering our companies' participation in the cross-border market and will provide a significant boost to the internal market.
The new provisions highlight the information to be provided to consumers, requiring that such information be clear, comprehensible, and supplied in a timely manner.
It is also important that the burden of proof regarding the compliance with information requirements be shifted to the trader.

With regard to contracts concluded at a distance, particularly those entered into by telephone or via the internet, rules governing them should be established, and consumers protected by simple and effective safeguards.

We are confident that, at the conclusion of the institutional negotiations, the new directive will contribute to making the European Union more competitive and dynamic, as required in a global economy.

Madam President, dear colleagues, Commissioner Stefan Füle, the historic moment we are experiencing demands firm and determined action.
Like many of you, we observe the events unfolding in the Mediterranean basin with a mixture of hope, concern, and anticipation.

I feel, nevertheless, satisfied with the proactive stance the Parliament is preparing to adopt regarding the review of the European Union's southern neighborhood policy, which I hope will lead to a stronger presence of the Union in this region—not merely as a financier, but equally as a committed partner.
From the outset, by asserting the absolute necessity of defending our foundational values in relations with neighboring countries.
We can no longer compromise in the defense of democracy, nor in the defense of human rights, and least of all in the defense of social justice.

We can no longer prioritize short-term stability at the expense of the higher interests of citizens and their steadfast defense of individual and collective freedom, with particular attention to women's rights.
In the future, the Union must prioritize a bottom-up approach in its neighborhood policy.
It is essential that this takes place.
Only the greater involvement of local communities and civil society will ensure maximum effectiveness in its implementation.

But I cannot refrain from expressing a sense of frustration. I regret that this Parliament and the Commission are unwilling to seize this opportunity to clearly distinguish the eastern neighborhood policy—targeting countries that may potentially become our future partners in the Union—from the southern neighborhood policy.
I also challenge the Commission to demonstrate, in its 20 April review process, the ambition that the current moment demands of a neighbourhood policy.
Tailored to each state, with clear benchmarks and careful assessment, enabling progress toward a future Mediterranean economic space alongside the new democracies of the south.

To conclude, Mr Commissioner, we trust that the positive atmosphere and cooperation that have characterized work on this issue will lead to the European Parliament's ongoing involvement in the planning and evaluation of this policy. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, the reports we have discussed and which I today congratulate the rapporteurs on reinforce a set of initiatives whose ambition is to revitalise the internal market.

increases in imports from third countries of aquaculture and fisheries products to the European market and a consequent abandonment of the activity due to financial difficulties, resulting in further job losses.

Mr. President, Commissioner, I would like to begin by saying that, of course, I consider the agreement reached for Portugal to be a balanced one.
It is a very harsh agreement for the populations, but it fundamentally focuses on structural reforms aimed at increasing growth and competitiveness.
And I swear that this is very positive.

I do not adopt here the stance of others who claim that countries such as Greece, Ireland, and Portugal did everything correctly, made no mistakes, did not overreach, did everything well, and that it was solely the international crisis that caused the problems.

No, our governments erred and pursued incorrect policies, but this does not mean that there is not now, within the context of the Union, an opportunity to once again have the possibility of returning to the right path and the correct course.

Therefore, it is important to emphasize here that, in fact, one must inform public opinions opposed to supporting countries in difficulty that they are not actually giving money to these countries, but merely lending money to them.

Perhaps now is the time for the European Commission, the Fund, and the IMF to review their interest rates, so as to enable sustained growth in these countries currently facing difficulties by lowering those rates.

Madam President, Commissioner, my first words are to commend the work carried out by the rapporteur, my colleague Giorgio Stravakakis, who has done an outstanding job in identifying future synergies for enhanced effectiveness among the various structural fronts.

Your report also contributes to this joint debate on cohesion policy, a discussion that assumes greater significance not only because of the substance of the topic, but especially due to the timing of its consideration.
Indeed, we are currently in the process of examining the Commission's proposal, not only with regard to the new financial perspectives, but also concerning the future cohesion policy beyond 2013.
Cohesion policy is a fundamental policy for the European Union.

It is a horizontal policy that, through its excellence instruments—territorial funds—finances projects and programmes in Member States, aiming to reduce disparities between the development levels of various regions.
Experience, however, has demonstrated the difficulties in terms of efficiency and outcomes resulting from the fragmentation and complexity of this policy.

That a single strategic framework for the various funds, with an integrated, results-oriented approach, is the starting point for greater and better synergies among the various instruments and for strengthening their effectiveness.
It is also essential for better coordination among various sectoral policies and for greater efficiency in the use of resources.
We must seize the danger of crisis to seek new alternatives.

It is certain that all these reports we are discussing here today will pose immense challenges, and we know that to meet these challenges we also need a sound budget; however, we must make a clear distinction between budgetary aspects and political objectives.
The political objectives are well known, and indeed, it is by affirming these political objectives that we shall be able to offer a future to the regions.

And if that is the case, then we must place the budget at the service of the future of regions and citizens, rather than placing citizens and regions at the service of the budget. Thank you very much.
Mr. President, cohesion policy is the key policy for the future of the European project.
The construction of a common space, with an internal market and a shared currency, requires, in order to function effectively, a certain level of convergence among its member states and regions.
Current competitiveness disparities and development asymmetries have inevitably led to the weakening of the European space and to the crisis affecting the eurozone.
That is why this vital need for convergence absolutely requires a strong cohesion budget that cannot be weakened to finance other policies.
The European Union, in order to do more, must have more, and this can only be achieved by growing its budget, not by reducing it abruptly, even in the case of the cohesion budget.

I therefore ask the Commissioner whether the establishment of the Infrastructure Fund and the new innovation policies, which will be linked to cohesion and regional development, could not, without an increase in the community budget, turn into the Trojan horse introduced into the cohesion budget.

Modernization and renewal of energy infrastructure are essential factors for achieving the sustainable and smart growth objectives we pursue under the 2020 Strategy and for our energy security, which we require.
It is essential to involve all regions in this challenge.

To achieve this, the incentives provided by regulatory systems must be improved, particularly those applicable to higher-risk projects and more innovative initiatives, such as those being developed in the Azores islands in the field of renewable energy.

Regions should be associated with the phases of project approval and implementation, in defining priorities, as well as in their preparation and execution, as this ensures better outcomes and thus a stronger defense of the European interest.
The current economic situation further underscores the need to adopt an innovative and integrated approach to energy issues, taking into account all economic, environmental, and social aspects.
It is essential not to lose sight of its medium- and long-term effects, striving to ensure all European citizens access to safe, sustainable, and affordable energy.

Mr. President, the report we are debating is of particular importance, given the influence that general interest social services have on the daily lives of European citizens, as well as on social cohesion, employment creation, and economic growth.

Collaboration with rapporteur Prónces de Rossa was highly beneficial and clarifying, as the rapporteur himself just stated, enabling the adoption of several compromises in the Committee on Employment and Social Affairs.
Given the outcome we currently have here and are discussing, I commend and congratulate the rapporteur.
In fact, on our part, the main concern was to make clear that responsibilities in this matter lie with the Member States.
National authorities are responsible for defining, organizing, and financing social services of general interest.
Thus, there is no need to create harmonizing legislation at the European level, given the great diversity of these general interest social services across the 27 Member States.

One need only look at health, social security, employment, and social housing services, which serve as examples demonstrating that proximity and knowledge of local contexts are fundamental to the proper and fair delivery of these services.

Mr. President, as rapporteur for the REGI opinion on the future of international social services, I would like to congratulate my colleague, Rossa, highlighting the fact that we are facing an essential pillar of the social market economy model we advocate for Europe, in which the SIEG plays a fundamental role in ensuring its cohesion.

Social cohesion at the level of the European Union advises against overlooking the existence of minimum protection standards in all member states, without prejudice to respect for the competences of their national, regional, and local authorities.

European regions play a very important role in providing these services to populations, and their views must be taken into account, given the inherent constraints they face, in order to ensure the provision of these services to the populations.

In a crisis situation such as the one we are currently experiencing, international social services serve as a social safety net for the most disadvantaged or for those whose incomes do not allow them access to private services.

It is essential to ensure that member states, when decentralizing powers, also provide the corresponding financial resources, so that regional and local authorities can effectively deliver services to the population.

NATO and the United Nations have exceeded all resolutions, even those adopted by the United Nations Security Council.
It is this silence regarding Libya, this condemnation, that we must express—and it is precisely because this is absent from the present resolution that I cannot endorse it.

Thank you, Mr. President. I begin by congratulating the rapporteur and all those who contributed to achieving this work.
There is no doubt that this report constitutes and will constitute a reference point regarding recommendations and solid, coordinated responses to the challenges posed by the crisis.
If there is one conclusion that can be drawn, it is that the crisis we are experiencing will only be overcome through the deepening of European integration.

In a period of severe financial, economic, and social difficulties, and of painful austerity measures, in some member states, such as my country, Portugal, there is a temptation to retreat into a protective and nationalist stance.
But, Gisele, I make mistakes.

The European response to the crisis requires a genuine European economic governance that pursues sustainable growth, promotes job creation, and develops the social market economy, providing long-term solutions with clearly defined objectives to which member states can relate.
The strategy for exiting the crisis needs to be financed with a sufficiently ambitious community budget.
To prevent a new sovereign debt and euro crisis, closer coordination in fiscal policy is required, along with the establishment of a public debt issuance system and euro bonds.

Madam President, Commissioner, Presidency in office, it is commonplace to say that crises help strengthen Europe.
Historically, this was the case, but today I am no longer so certain that this remains true.

The slow, improvised, reactive manner in which the Union is responding to the Greek crisis worsens the situation every day and spreads fear and loss of confidence in the Union and its currency in a frightening manner.
This, despite the problems of the eurozone stemming from obvious flaws in the construction of the single currency, which today focus on its most vulnerable links.

For the current crisis to strengthen the Union, a strategic vision is required—such as existed in the past—and which, unfortunately, neither the Commission nor the Council have managed to impart to the European programme and project.
However, this report from the Parliament gives us this vision: more Europe, not less, is the solution.
The Parliament recalls that the market and the single currency generate intolerable divergences among economies and that these must be counteracted by specific policies.

It acknowledges that the functioning of the euro requires a strengthened European budget backed by own resources, and calls for a European debt issuance agency capable of protecting Member States from market speculation.
and provide them with credit at reasonable prices and terms. It also acknowledges that diplomas and the mobility of workers between countries require mutual recognition of social rights and pensions.
The Parliament has united to send clear messages; let us hope the Commission and the Council listen to them.

Mr. President, the number of people without shelter, as well as those living below the poverty line, continues to rise in several Member States in recent years.
The economic, financial, and social crisis and the rise in unemployment have worsened this problem, as everyone has already diagnosed.
We also all agree that this phenomenon represents a denial of important values of the European Union, respect for human dignity and respect for fundamental rights.

In my country, for example, which is subject to a stringent austerity policy, the concept of homelessness, according to the national plan, encompasses people who go to seek help in order to have a meal.
The number of people seeking help for eating disorders has increased by more than 20% since 2008.
An increase is observed among families and individuals in need who have experienced a change in their living conditions due to unemployment and also due to bank debt.
This is merely an example illustrating the seriousness of this situation.
Last year, the European Parliament adopted a written declaration on an EU strategy for the homeless.

With this statement, this Parliament has highlighted the need for the European Union to establish a strategy supported by national and regional strategies, with the long-term objective of ending the situations of people sleeping on the streets and the prolonged state of homelessness.
National strategies should focus on preventive measures, as well as concrete actions to improve the quality of services provided to the homeless and the availability of affordable housing.
It is also evident that this European strategy must be compatible with the social housing policies of the member states.

We have debated the peace process in the Middle East, but is there some ongoing process that has escaped my attention?
Unfortunately, no.

The European Union repeatedly voices, as does the majority of the international community, the need for two peoples and two states sharing the space between the Jordan River and the Mediterranean, each having the right to aspire to live in peace and security.
But without dialogue between the parties, entrenching themselves in a cycle of hatreds and misunderstandings that has persisted for 63 years, only extremism and frustration are nurtured.

It is essential that Europe and the Arab League become much more actively involved in seeking a negotiated solution for the borders, based on those existing in 1967, with adjustments allowing the majority of Jewish settlements to remain within Israel.

May the issue of Jerusalem, the holy city and future capital of the two states, be clarified and mutually accepted as a symbol of peaceful coexistence between two peoples who bear the responsibility of fulfilling the prophecy of Isaiah that this is the land of milk and honey.

That all EU countries participate in resolving the issue of Palestinian refugees, and that the international community, and in particular the Arab nation, participate in the necessary financial compensation.

It remains absolutely essential that a military force, under the auspices of the United Nations and including participation from American and Islamic forces, among others, be deployed on the ground as guarantor of peacekeeping.
Thus there is courage and political will.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, tourism requires a clear and coordinated European approach, with careful attention to the potentials and challenges that the various tourism modes offer us.
The tourism diversity offered by the European Union is so rich that it enables exponential growth in this sector and enhances the Union's competitiveness itself.

A bet on this key sector, which accounts for 10% of GDP and approximately 12% of employment in the European Union, will not only create European added value but also help address the economic crisis we are currently facing.
It comes from an overseas region, Madeira, where tourism is the main economic activity.

they did not in any way contribute to alleviating tensions in the region, nor did they contribute to the genuine pursuit of a life of peace, security, and dignity for the two peoples, who have the right to live together harmoniously.

I find it truly shameful that we can account for only a few hundred—the number of refugees received in Europe.

And Europe.

This set of measures, and perhaps an agreement among states—the thing I have referred to as a kind of parasocial agreement going beyond the Treaties and capable, from this moment onward, of setting all these ideas in motion.

A swift reintegration of workers into the labor market is a priority objective.

An example of the usefulness of this fund is my country, Portugal, which has accessed it four times; although we are aware that access to this fund could be improved, particularly in countries most affected by the crisis.

Madam President, Commissioner, this is a good report by our colleague Casa.
First of all, one must ask why revising VAT in the European Union is necessary.
First, there is the need to align with the single market.
The more regimes and types of VAT there are, the less fluid the market will be.
A VAT reform benefits e-commerce, focuses competition on the intrinsic value and base price of goods and services, and not on fiscal differences.
Combats fraud, ensures trust—essential especially in cross-border transactions—improves the economy of SMEs, and reduces administrative burdens.
What other measures concerning Joventut are already underway in the single market that could be linked to VAT?
First of all, a simple, clear, and transparent digital support mechanism, Measure 7 of the single market.
Measure 5 on services, the need to review the legislation on standardization, and the implementation of the Directive
The new measure aims to remove fiscal barriers and explicitly review the VAT system and VAT equation in online and face-to-face commerce, which we all experience when ordering books.
The pressures for VAT reform stem from two fundamental grounds.
First of all, the economic crisis.
Additional revenues are needed, including, in some countries, reducing payroll contributions to promote economic development, shifting this burden to VAT.

Secondly, the second major source of pressure is population aging, demographic aging, and the trend of shifting from indirect taxes to direct taxes, due to the reduction in the population base subject to taxation.
It is necessary to reconsider the balance between this direct and indirect taxation.
Finally, we must, in these reforms, reconcile subsidiarity with harmonization.
It is necessary to narrow the range of variation in the standard VAT rate so that we can all understand it more clearly.
Greater transparency is needed in defining the reduced VAT rate and its scope of application, along with a clear and widely disseminated list of reduced rates.
It is necessary to examine exemption thresholds for SMEs, keeping in mind that each exemption inevitably brings with it a series of minor or major frauds.
Finally, welcome the Commission's recent proposal to establish a European VAT.

The advantages of public procurement are certainly well known, but the obstacles are also evident—conflicts of interest between European legislation and the objectives of member states undermine competitiveness.
The dichotomy in price quality and the complexity of laws and procedures are evident.
It is essential to examine existing best practices, particularly in Portugal, where electronic procurement has been functioning well and has facilitated public procurement.
An effective European public procurement represents a privileged means of controlling public deficit and fostering greater growth.
To achieve this, it must have a stable and predictable legal framework, less bureaucratic procedures to attract SMEs, and the protection of social and environmental values.

That is why I express my satisfaction with the intentions that Commissioner Barnier has just presented to us regarding the revision of public procurement this year, as one of the twelve priorities of the Single Market Act.
to congratulate colleague Rula and all those who collaborated with her.

Only a few countries, such as my own, Portugal, have succeeded in this area.

The advantages of electronic public procurement are, moreover, evident and have already been outlined here. It enables replacing costly access procedures with the simple consultation of the electronic platform, that is, it significantly reduces costs for companies, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises.

Small and medium-sized enterprises, online dissemination, and the ease of receiving procedural documents expand the number of suppliers, increase competition, and enable better purchasing, thus transforming the public procurement process

easier and more flexible, it simplifies access to European Union public contracts, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises, thereby facilitating their participation in the public procurement market—key drivers of the economy.

In this regard, we welcome and encourage the European Commission for strengthening the mechanisms aimed at making electronic public procurement an increasingly comprehensive reality. Thank you very much.

We are living through decisive hours for Europe's fate, and although the situation has swiftly, before everyone's eyes, evolved from a problem confined to Greece or Portugal into a major European issue, the truth is that the inadequacy of the proposed solutions and their delay have failed to halt the rapid deterioration of the situation.
The search for solutions tailored to individual states, at the expense of genuinely European solutions, allowed markets to continue speculating on sovereign debts and imposed deadlines and rhythms
It is absolutely impossible for some states to achieve through measures of an unbearable brutality applied to individuals, businesses, and the public sector.
A common solution is absolutely imperative, as already found for the risk of European sovereign debts, which removes market pressure and speculation and provides time for the necessary adjustment.
Without this, what we will have at the end are countries that continue to have nothing left to pay their creditors, economies destroyed, and immense suffering.

To my fellow shadow rapporteurs, with whom I have had a fruitful collaboration, and also to the rapporteurs of the partners from the four involved committees. These partners have made a valuable contribution to this report.
We received 400 amendments and developed 47 commitments, all of which were approved.
This demonstrates the importance and interest generated by this topic of new skills and jobs.
Ultimately, it is about preparing Europe's human resources, anticipating the needs of the labor market over the next decade.
The key strategy in preparing the report was to rephrase common ideas to avoid repetition and ensure the report's coherence.
I believe that this goal has been achieved.
We all know that the crisis currently affecting us has revealed the weaknesses of the European economic and social model, as well as nullified many of the advances achieved in the past.
Since the first effects of the crisis began to be felt in labor markets in 2008, Europe has already lost more than five million jobs.
Today, nearly 23 million people are unemployed.
Youth unemployment is even more alarming, as the youth unemployment rate is near 21 and represents more than double the overall unemployment rate.
On the other hand, in all member states, unemployment rates vary according to qualification levels.
This means that people with higher qualifications find it easier to secure employment.
We also know that, in the near future, demands regarding skills, competencies, and qualifications will increase across all types and levels of profession.
In this context, Europe faces four challenges: first, job creation; second, the need to develop and remain competitive.
Then, the sustainability of social security systems, and finally, the difficulty in finding individuals with the appropriate skills to fill job vacancies.

This report thus has a dual objective: first, to emphasize the need for swift action in addressing skill gaps across Europe; second, to enable us to better anticipate labor market needs in terms of skills.

To this end, several measures are highlighted. First, to ensure the availability of qualified labor, it is necessary to improve basic skills, as well as competencies in science, innovation, information and communication technologies, and foreign languages.
As I have already mentioned, competence needs must be identified as early as possible, within a timeframe of at least ten years.
Today, the potential of a new sustainable economy, the green economy, and the need for investment in ecological qualifications are well recognized.
Subsequently, participation in lifelong learning, professional requalification, and the reduction of school dropouts should be increased.
Another important issue is the mutual recognition of diplomas and professional qualifications, and the urgency of advancing this recognition.
Fourth, we draw attention to the connection that must exist between educational institutions and the needs of the labor market.
Interns must be of high quality and supervised by mentors, so as to enable them to transition into employment and prevent any potential abuses arising from their internship status.
Particular emphasis is also placed on the role of social partners and social dialogue.
The report also proposes measures to promote demand and job creation.
We have a future ahead of us, and we must create jobs.
Small and medium-sized enterprises play a decisive role in achieving this objective.

It is also necessary to strengthen the single market to facilitate the free movement of workers, and thus the need to coordinate social security systems so that workers can have their acquired rights guaranteed, particularly the portability of pensions.

Lastly, the issue of labor market flexibility. To improve the functioning of the labor market, flexicurity rules must be adapted to the socioeconomic contexts of each Member State. Thank you very much.

Madam President, what we have been told today in this statement by the Council is that, for technical reasons, democracy would have to be sacrificed in the context of the European Semester. But what we are discussing here is a political issue, and the question I pose is whether political coordination can possibly exist without democracy.
Where, after all, is the scrutiny of the European Parliament?
Where is the scrutiny of national parliaments over their major budgetary choices?

But if the issue were technical, as has been stated here, then we are discussing a variable technique, because we know that the Council substantially eased the conditions for the four countries on the waiting list—France, Italy, Hungary, and Spain—whereas it did not do the same for the countries under rescue programmes.
Consequently, what kind of intergovernmentality is this that allows Germany to give opinions about Portugal, but does not allow Portugal to give opinions about Germany?
And, Mr. President, I kindly ask you only to pay attention: no problem of democratic legitimacy is solved by asking Members of Parliament to consult information on the Internet.
This is a problem of infra-exclusion, not a problem of democratic exclusion. That is precisely what we are discussing here. Thank you very much.
Madam President, Commissioner, colleagues, a first word to congratulate rapporteur Dardenne on the quality and timeliness of this report, and also Commissioner Andor for the initiatives announced.
Poverty is indeed a very serious issue, requiring effective and swift action to halt the growing phenomenon, which currently affects more than 80 million Europeans.
The severe crisis we are already experiencing has worsened this situation and made the times we live in dramatic, as its commissioner Andor stated.
The number of people unemployed continues to rise, and we all know that unemployment is a gateway to poverty and social exclusion.
This situation requires resources, willpower, and great commitment from the European Commission and the member states.
If this does not happen, the goal of lifting 20 million European citizens out of poverty during this decade will not be achieved.
Therefore, I warmly welcome the initiative of the Platform against Poverty, which is a very important instrument for ensuring social justice.
But no one doubts that the best remedies against poverty and social exclusion are job creation and the promotion of economic growth.
Access to the Internet should today be regarded as a new fundamental right of citizens, and not merely as a commodity, because this right is essential to freedom of expression.
they depend on essential factors such as freedom of expression, access to and sharing of information, access to and sharing of culture and knowledge, as well as democratic and media pluralism.

That is why this access cannot be subject to speculative tariffs or contracts that restrict free choice and the ease of switching providers, particularly in the field of mobile telecommunications, as we well know.
Active citizenship therefore requires that complete and transparent information be provided.
The blocking of internet access is not exclusive to political regimes that fear their populations.
It may also occur as a result of economic choices, such as offering privileges to select customers and providing less equitable treatment to those who are less profitable.
That is why I support the Parliament in clearly positioning itself in favor of Internet neutrality and the preservation of its integrity.
Let us speak the truth.
We have been taking the medication for three years to get out of the crisis, yet the situation worsens day by day.
Instead of healing, we created two vicious and fatal cycles.
The first among banks and states.
In Europe, it is today easier to discuss the bankruptcy of a state than that of a major bank, yet there remains an expectation that banks finance states and that states rescue their banks.
This spiral must be broken.
The Commission must advance European legislation that protects taxpayers from bank crises.
But it is also vital to protect state debt from attacks by speculators.

And for this purpose, it is urgent and long overdue to prepare coordinated issuances of sovereign debt—whether called Eurobonds or Stability Bonds—guaranteed through robust, community-based instruments rather than intergovernmental ones.
In short, we also need to reconsider the involvement of the European Central Bank.
But the second vicious circle stems from the argument that some states are rich because they are virtuous, while others are poor because they have sinned.
This narrative is politically explosive and technically incorrect.

She ignores the essential fact that a single market and a single currency, built by political choice and among unequal partners, necessarily exacerbate competitiveness imbalances between the center and the periphery.
And this is what must be drawn in economic governance.
No, sir Rompoi, through sanctions that promote the crushing of wages and incomes, with one country exerting control over another.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, the integrated maritime policy advocates a coordinated approach to the management and governance of oceans, seas, and coasts, within a global and coherent framework of all European policies related to the sea.

Having a global perspective and an integrated approach implies the coordination of European policies related to the sea and calls for improved governance through greater involvement in the European decision-making process.
The success and relevance of this policy have been demonstrated over recent years through various initiatives funded in the form of preparatory actions and pilot projects.
Integrated maritime policy must therefore continue to receive appropriate funding over the coming years of the current financial programming period.

In this regard, I advocate for adequate funding up to 2013, not only to consolidate previous projects, but also support the establishment of a stable framework regarding the objectives of this program.

Issues related to policy, governance, sustainability, and maritime surveillance are, for obvious reasons, particularly relevant to certain regions of the European Union, such as the overseas territories.

The RUP are regions, except for French Guinea, which are small in size and population, dependent on a limited number of economic activities, and where maritime and fishing activities assume particular importance.
Moreover, the outermost regions contribute to making the European Union possess the world's largest exclusive economic zone.

The RUPs account for more than half of the European Union's exclusive economic zone, approximately 15 out of 25 million square kilometers, and, together with coastal regions, are fundamental to the economic growth of the European Union and its member states.

A program such as the one outlined in the integrated maritime policy should benefit these regions by prioritizing transnational coordination of maritime policies and relevant international cooperation.
It is therefore necessary to ensure continuous and sufficient funding, not only for the next two years, but also and above all for the upcoming financial framework starting in 2014.
Failing to seize the opportunity when discussing integrated maritime policy to highlight the immense potential that the Atlantic and insular dimensions hold for the European Union.

The Atlantic, as Europe's western maritime frontier, also establishes its connection to North and South America and to Africa, imparting a strategic dimension essential for Europe within the framework of a maritime policy designed with a forward-looking perspective.

And it is with the future in mind that the integrated maritime policy, beyond promoting the coordination of maritime activities, must place at the forefront of its priorities the potential of the Atlantic in its three-dimensional dimension, based on science and technology.

The investigation of the Atlantic crisis, the deep sea, and the seafloor represents a new frontier whose potential lies in biotechnology and mineral resources, and which holds incalculable value for the knowledge-based economy.
The Union should therefore take advantage of the Azores' privileged position and the presence of its oceanographic department to establish a deep-sea observatory there.
Enough of this path.
When the diagnosis is wrong, the medications don't work.
And in the case of the eurozone, its remedies have brought it all the way to the brink of death.
The successive miracle solutions that were proposed were likewise discredited by the markets.
the Financial Stabilization Fund, the successive tightening measures of the Stability and Growth Pact, the revenues from the troika in countries below the program.
Blind discipline, without growth, triggered a recessive wave across economies that were fully interconnected, thus contaminating them with bankruptcies and unemployment.
And in the hands of the markets—that is, of rating agencies and speculators—governments are falling one after another, and solvent countries are becoming insolvent.

Amending the Treaties now is another step on this disastrous path, an additional risk that could destroy what remains of national sovereignty, placing the countries in the hands of the European Court of Justice.
No, it is not necessary to amend the treaties.
Two decisions must be made in this emergency situation we are facing.
First of all, a genuine agenda for sustained growth and cohesion must be implemented.
Where is the Agenda 2020?
Where is the restart budget?
Where are the so-called project bonds for Europe's structural investments?
And, secondly, sovereign debt must be protected.
Naturally, aerobonios are necessary, but in this emergency we are facing, it is urgent that the European Central Bank, directly or indirectly, defend sovereign debt against speculators.
Not in a shameful manner, but with a firm, resolute stance, as befits any central bank defending its currency and the economy that supports it.
For this, it is not necessary to amend the Treaties; what is needed is political will and European conviction.
We will move forward with the Treaty once we have drawn all the lessons from the current crisis, and there are certainly many lessons to be drawn, but this is certainly not the moment.

Mr. President, I believe we are at a stage of assessment regarding these crisis policies, austerity measures that have affected so many countries, as well as the so-called structural reforms.
And it is within this context that I believe we cannot fail to recognize how much social commotion they have generated.
And it has generated social commission not only in countries facing greater difficulties, in southern countries, not only in so-called hotspots, but also in other contexts across Europe.
Last week alone, Portugal experienced the largest general strike ever, involving both the public and private sectors, a strike that took to the streets.

But if we look at what has happened today in the United Kingdom, we see that we have witnessed the largest strike ever, especially considering that people took to the streets as vigorously as they did in Portugal.
We expect a large demonstration in Brussels on Friday.

What this demonstrates, Mr. President, as do the strikes in people taking to the streets, is that the causes and problems we have been facing are common, and that is precisely why the response is also common.
The crisis of the European Union is spreading to all member states, reaching incredible proportions.

We have witnessed chaos in financial markets and the growing inability to secure essential financing for the operations of our small and medium-sized enterprises, even those that are healthy, driving them to desperation, bankruptcy, and unemployment.
It is a spiral that fuels recession and is destroying the economy and society.

No one denies the strict necessity of budgetary discipline, deep restructuring, and greater economic governance, but these are necessary conditions that, in the short term, are no longer sufficient to reverse the ongoing deterioration within the Union.

And if it is true that policies stimulating employment and growth are needed, without which we will never manage to control sovereign debts, the central issue today is ensuring the financing of our businesses and economies, without which the Union will plunge into crisis.
Therefore, more than ever, more than treaty revisions, we today need a strong and decisive intervention by the European Central Bank.

Welcome to this Parliament, Mr. Draghi.
We are living through special and dramatic circumstances, and they require determined and urgent measures.

The spiral between state debt and bank debt must be broken, but at this moment the priority is defending the states, ensuring that eurozone countries and the euro itself are not destroyed by credit rating agencies and speculators.
In the report we will approve, Tremosa and Baselt propose ideas to rethink the architecture of the euro zone, create eurobonds and a European treasury, but all of this takes time.

By prioritizing relations with the general population and, in particular, with civil society organizations, we will be enhancing the objectivity and effectiveness of our neighborhood policy, which is intended to be founded exclusively on the universal values we uphold and share.
I emphasize, however, that the European Union does not intend to impose a model or a predefined formula for political reforms in third countries.

The neighborhood policy is now based on upholding the pursuit of shared values and a commitment to democracy, human rights, the rule of law, and good governance.

Our partners are called upon to assume greater responsibility in the process; therefore, we advocate the principles of greater differentiation, greater conditionality, and more for more.

Mr. President, fiscal consolidation is imposing almost unbearable demands on some European citizens.
However, each day fewer people are left bearing a greater burden.
The tax burden falls on workers, small and medium-sized enterprises, and consumers, while at the same time witnessing completely disproportionate tax evasion and tax flight.
The Group of European Socialists wanted to know how much this tax evasion and tax fraud amounts to across the twenty-seven countries, and it amounts to one trillion euros.
This is more than the countries of the European Union spend on health.

This means a volume such that, if we spent only one-fifth of this amount on investment, investment in Europe would rise from 2.7% of GDP to 3.5%. We are therefore discussing an exceptionally high value.

The meaning of the resolution we are about to adopt, initiated by this Parliament, is precisely a very clear demand on the Commission and the Council to accelerate concrete action against tax evasion and fraud.

This means, in particular, concrete initiatives for tax cooperation within the European Union, as well as tax cooperation with our neighboring countries, in particular Switzerland, but negotiated by the Commission and not on a bilateral, country-by-country basis.
This means reforming and harmonizing accounting rules, and enhancing transparency and reporting.
This clearly means reactivating the fundamental issue of combating tax fraud and tax evasion through tax havens.
Europe is not a lesser partner.
Europe is a major partner in the international context.
Europe has the obligation, within the G20 framework, to clearly advocate a joint fight against fraud and tax evasion.

Without this, we lose credibility with our citizens, and therefore, my final words in this debate are a call for the Commission and the Council to launch an effective fight against fraud and tax evasion.

Mr. President, I begin by congratulating my colleague, Ms. Nybler, on her excellent work, and I underscore the significance that, on this Europe Day which we are celebrating today, we are debating and will approve tomorrow measures that align with the legitimate aspirations of our citizens.

What is at stake in this regulation is imposing limits on mobile phone charges imposed on European consumers when roaming, with its main objective being to eliminate roaming within the European space starting from 2016.
Our citizens will now benefit from a significant reduction in prices regarding calls, SMS messages, and data.
If today there are operators charging €3 per megabyte, from July the maximum price they can charge will be 70 cents per megabyte, dropping to 20 cents per megabyte in 2014.
Regarding incoming calls, the maximum price that may be charged will be eight cents this year and five cents in 2014.
In July 2012, a family with two mobile phone users will save, as the lady commissioner said, 200 euros on their bill.
The second benefit is improved quality of information for consumers.
Starting from summer, operators will be required to inform consumers about the prices charged when they travel outside the European Union as well.
Until now, the obligation applied only within the European space.
Third advantage: the possibility of more operators entering the market, which means increased competition, stimulating better services and lower prices.

Finally, in the near future, the consumer may, if desired, have two contracts—one for services within the national territory and another for services abroad—while retaining the same mobile number and receiving only a single bill.
The consumer, our citizen, will likewise be given the opportunity to contract with any European operator that offers them the best price.
This report is the perfect example of the Europe of citizens, the Europe of freedom of movement and freedom of establishment. Many thanks.

Project bonds are one of the possible sources.

Third point: the European Stability Mechanism and the European Central Bank must prepare to actually function as genuine firewalls in defending European sovereign debt.

It is one billion euros held up within the banking system, while thousands of viable businesses die every day due to lack of funding.
Fifth point: investment makes no sense without search.
