exit this crisis without promoting growth, particularly in those states most affected by the financial crisis. But sometimes, when I hear some of my colleagues here, I get the impression that they believe growth can continue despite excessive deficits and growing public accounts.

Now, we must continue to insist on spending reductions and structural reforms. We can do everything possible on the growth side, but we cannot abandon the path of controlling and reducing public expenditure.
Greater growth cannot be more relaxed, more restful; greater growth and growth-oriented policies must go hand in hand with great rigor in public finances and great rigor in structural reforms.
If we begin speaking only of growth and start believing in magical solutions to growth, we will once again end up with an unsustainable public debt and continue without structural reforms.

Mr. President, thank you, Ms. Podimata. Today, for the first time, we are discussing here the financial transaction tax, and its approval would be the first signal that speculation will not go entirely unpunished in the crisis we are living through.
This House and the European institutions must be accountable to people, not to financial markets.
For decades, financial markets' lobbies managed to govern the world without opposition and without any problems.
It is obvious to us that during 2008 and 2012, the crisis in the speculative financial market lay at the root of most of the problems we experienced.

And yet, these speculative financial markets managed not only to ensure that nothing was done, but also to effect a direct transfer from the pockets of citizens straight into these very speculative markets.

We have been fighting for this for many years, Mr. President, but it was only when peoples across much of Europe began to say enough was enough that the institutions governing the European Union finally took some initiative.
And therefore, this is the response we must give to citizens; it is a first signal we must send.
The citizens of Europe, who have everything to offer in return, deserve not to be disrespected.
And therefore, this proposal is not complete, not perfect, and will not solve the entire problem of financial speculation, but it is an important signal.
We have been going, we haven't, European leaders have been apologizing to financial markets.

Mr. President, the financial transactions tax should function as a means of controlling and discouraging high-risk speculative activities in the financial sector.

The Podimata report sought to align the European Commission's initial proposal with the well-known UK stamp duty. In this way, we made the proposal more appealing to hesitant member states.

Mr. President, European leaders continue to insist on budgetary discipline, and I ask: budgetary discipline—very well, let's pursue it. Because what exactly are we talking about when we speak of budgetary discipline?

In my understanding, fiscal discipline means honoring contracts with pensioners and not abandoning the State's commitments midway. In my understanding, fiscal discipline means respecting the contracts of workers and not inventing extraordinary taxes or cutting subsidies.

The budgetary objective is to respect commitments in health, education, and culture, and not to close services or halt and cancel projects midway. Discipline means putting banks in order, rather than continually drawing funds from taxpayers to place in the hands of speculators in order to save banks.
This is the budgetary discipline I would like to see implemented in the European Union, rather than continuing this feast of the markets.

Mr. President, does it need an example? The budget currently being discussed in Portugal, under the influence of the troika, is a perfect example: it violates every commitment any state should have toward its citizens, in order to uphold secret agreements that are ruining Europe and its countries.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Commissioner, it is a privilege to present the report on the 20 concerns of citizens and businesses in
Regarding the functioning of the single market, precisely in this debate with Commissioner Barnier, marking the twentieth anniversary of this market.

We are well aware and proud that, over these 20 years, the single market has been the driving force behind major economic and employment opportunities across Europe, transforming the lives of European citizens and European businesses.
Traveling, residing, and working in the spaces of the Union are freedoms available to all citizens of the 27 member states.
For European consumers, the single market means greater freedom of choice and lower prices.
For our businesses, the single market means greater business opportunities, with access to 500 million consumers, which translates into more economic growth and more job creation.
To illustrate the progress the single market has achieved in Europe since its creation in 1992 until the onset of the crisis,
In 2008, it is sufficient to consider the 2.7 million jobs created and the additional 2.13% growth in GDP.
Today, taking a TOEFL or receiving a TOEFL score while traveling in Europe is cheaper by 13% than it was in 2005.
The freedom of movement of people finds its most eloquent expression in the Erasmus programme.
Over 2.5 million students have benefited from this European exchange program in education and mobility.
Over the past five years, companies have also benefited from a 25% reduction in administrative positions due to the legislation of the single market.
But a question arises: is everything really going well in the single market?
No, not everything works well.
The economic and financial crisis affecting us has had negative repercussions on the functioning of this market.
Our citizens, in some countries, living through the hardship of unemployment and facing austerity measures, have developed a skeptical view of Europe and a negative perception of the internal market.
People still do not know or sufficiently understand their rights, nor do they know where to find information or assistance.
On the other hand, many Member States unjustifiably delay the transposition of European directives and legislation on the single market, thereby undermining their full potential.

In certain areas of citizens' daily lives, matters as simple as, for example, the reimbursement of medical expenses following a temporary stay in another EU country, or opening a bank account for a student in the Programme,

Erasmus, switching energy suppliers, transferring pension benefits, recognition of qualifications, and registering a vehicle in another Member State remain very complex, difficult, or costly procedures.
It is important to address these problems.
That was my concern, as rapporteur.
Therefore, the message conveyed in the report is very clear: more political will is needed, much more political will from the member states and European institutions.

Council, above all the Council and the Commission, to govern better and jointly, it is necessary to regain the trust of citizens and businesses, and it is essential to facilitate the exercise of their rights.

Madam President, Commissioner Barnier, and dear colleague Regina Bastos, my congratulations on your efforts, already so successfully achieved.
When Jacques Delors chaired the European Commission, the single market of the Union was established in 1992.
Delors then summarized the three fundamental principles of the single market: competition that stimulates, cooperation that strengthens, and solidarity that unites.
Twenty years later, the economic and financial crisis brought back the threat of national protectionism, and it became clear that the internal market is not a completed project.

And the celebration of these twenty years must be seized as an opportunity for relaunching the single market, with the concern of placing the market at the service of the citizen, rather than the citizen at the service of the market.
To this end, priorities must be established, and I would like to discuss one priority in particular.
It is essential to have a common policy to produce and distribute energy at affordable prices for consumers, with a high level of energy efficiency.
through a European energy market, served by cross-border infrastructure, with the potential for community leverage of investment that represents added European value.
In energy as well, Madam President, it will dominate the common market.
The greatest achievements of the European Union undoubtedly represent one of the strongest pillars in the development of the Union, its member states, and its regions.
However, for it to be effective and fair, its policies must emphasize equality of opportunity for citizens and all regions, promoting the internal cohesion of the Union.

In this context, it is essential to recognize that we can only achieve the greatest development of our most remote regions, such as the outermost regions, through their greater integration into the single market.

Regions such as the Azores need to continue enhancing their competitiveness by improving their competitive conditions, but we will never achieve this unless we resolve our accessibility issues in the transport sector.
We can achieve this through specific instruments that better address the problems raised by our detachment.

We therefore argue that the Commission should consider a specific programme, such as a transport-oriented POSEI, as an essential instrument for integrating this type of region into the internal market.
Thank you very much, Madam President. I begin by saying thank you very much for this excellent debate.

I welcome the presence of Minister Mavroyiannis and his participation in this debate; I thank all my colleagues for their constructive approach, and to some, their skeptical stance, which is understandable.

And thank you very much, Mr. Commissioner Barnier, for the dedication and enthusiasm you bring to the discussion of these issues, these challenges facing the internal market, and the political will to address them.
We still have much to answer to citizens.
Citizens are dissatisfied, disillusioned, and expect more from us, expect more from the Council, expect more from the Commission, expect more from the Member States.
This review is very useful; this review of the past 20 years is important. But, as many have said here, it is crucial to open the door to a look toward the future.
And the gaze toward the future passes through many issues raised here and through many measures already announced by the European Commission.
The digital single market, citizens' mobility, the reform of the EURES portal, and the European energy market are examples
eloquent testimony that we still have an important journey ahead in the future, and that this journey is of great importance and vital in addressing this era of crisis we are experiencing.
Citizens are suffering; citizens expect much from politicians and from the responses that politicians can provide to the adverse conditions they are enduring.
Therefore, it is important that the single market be one of the responses to the major challenge of competitiveness, employment, and economic growth.
I am confident that all of us, following this celebration of our 20th anniversary, are more committed than ever to providing the answers our citizens need.

six pack and the future two pack, an extraordinarily strong body that reinforces European control over the economic and budgetary policies of member countries.

Balance and the anticipation of control, along with national autonomy and legitimacy, are difficult to achieve and have declined to a level of imposition, particularly of discipline, and ultimately may even trigger a serious package of sanctions.

Regarding the TUPEC, I would like briefly to point out that it is important that the Council, which is currently absent here, grants its negotiators a negotiating mandate consistent with the ambition to swiftly conclude this package, this TUPEC.

But more European coordination, returning to this theme, means less control by countries over their own destiny, which requires a delicate balance, as well as increased demands regarding the quality of the recommendations made to countries across at least three or four dimensions.

First, it is essential to ensure coherence between medium- and long-term objectives, which include growth, employment, and real convergence, and short-term recommendations, which focus primarily on discipline and nominal convergence. Second, there must be coherence

Thirdly, we must agree that there is a minimum threshold of social, economic, and labor rights that must not be exceeded by these recommendations. Lastly, it is essential that the quality of the recommendations be assessed based on the results achieved, which is far from being the case thus far.

I have concluded, Madam President. I would simply like to thank Mr. Gozez for his spirit of collaboration and to hope that he will take yet another step in this spirit, so as to broaden the consensus around his report, and that this spirit will also continue in relation to next year.
It is true, as has been mentioned many times, that there are many things we do not know about shale gas extraction.
There are also many that we already know, and it is advisable not to forget them.
We know, for example, that each shale gas extraction operation uses more than 19 million liters of potable water, enough to supply one European citizen's annual needs.

We also know that, for each shale gas extraction carried out, in each operation, 300 cotton swabs are pumped into the ground, up to 300 tons of chemicals may be injected, and about 80 percent of these remain in the ground and never leave it.

We also know that, in each gas extraction process X, radioactive elements and heavy metals are released underground, and together with the evaporation of toxic residues resulting from the process, they constitute a real threat to public health.
This poses a risk of contamination of potable water resources, and it is a real and imminent contamination.

Knowing this, we realize that shale gas extraction is also a response that appears easy, focusing solely on the immediate, on industry and profit, while ignoring populations, the future, and the environment.

But this is also the Europe that knows, in this House, that water is an essential, vital, and important resource, that understands the need to protect citizens' health and must safeguard it, that seeks agreements to prevent emissions and promote energy efficiency, because we know that environmental standards are important, as are standards for health.
Therefore, when one does not know everything, being inside is the minimum.
And they may even want to do it, but do not do so in our name, nor in the name of the citizens who are here to represent us.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, indeed, the past years have shown us how the Economic and Monetary Union did not withstand the crisis.
We also showed that we had a European institutional architecture full of holes, in particular the institutional architecture of the euro.
That is why the report presented here and the proposal put forward are far ahead in terms of vision compared to the proposal submitted by the governments.

But also, as the rapporteur said, we are on ground where political choices must be made, and that is precisely why I would like to clarify the reasons why we have certain objections and why we will not be able to support this report.
The first one concerns issues related to conditions.

What we see and continue to experience is a narrative in which solidarity among different countries can only exist once everything has been fulfilled regarding the stability and growth program, regarding the intergovernmental agreement, the pact for
moreover, a set of measures that impose nothing other than massive austerity in the peripheries, and also a set of recessionary measures that, as we have already seen, are not working.
A second reason relates to banking union.

The rapporteur presents the banking supervision mechanism very well, but it is so essential that we cannot avoid the fundamental issue, and the fundamental issue here is being overlooked to the extent that we continue to add up.

We are aggregated within national systems, meaning we are all together in banking policy, we are all together in banking supervision, but we are all separate when something goes wrong, and thus taxpayers will continue to pay.
And a third point relates to the issue of common debt and the issuance of eurobonds.
Here, once again, the possibility of issuing common water is considered, but only when it is already too late.

Because we need common debt, and now it is no longer just a matter of correcting excessive deficits, because with the policies we are pursuing, these deficits will never be corrected; on the contrary, they will continue to worsen, to the despair of entire populations.

Therefore, Mr. President, I would like to conclude by saying that, despite all efforts, we have here a very clear example that together we could still rise, but divided, we will surely fall. And that is precisely why it was important to go even further.
Madam President, Ms. Ferreira, please, there is always room for improvement, but for Portugal, the support from the European Union over these past years has been fundamental.
Portugal has transformed from a country whose competitiveness was based on cheap labor into a country with highly skilled, highly sophisticated labor.
But when the country exports, it must have the necessary conditions in place.
Therefore, it is important that some countries with surpluses open their markets to the requalified exports of countries that started from a specialization profile that was absolutely unsustainable.

A final note to emphasize that it is not feasible for immigration to be the solution, that requalified labor, especially where support from the European Union has been utilized, should not now be lost through a large-scale export of the most qualified individuals to countries that have the capacity to receive them.
That is dramatic and completely undermines our future.

Mr. President, small-scale fishing deserves special protection under the Common Fisheries Policy, not only because it promotes socio-economic cohesion and ensures the livelihoods of many families, but also because it contributes to the stabilization of these communities along coastal areas.
Their economic and social sustainability must be a goal, ensuring them a differentiated fishing regime that grants them priority access to resources.
It enables the management of proximity and protects the fishing rights enjoyed by fishing professionals from these communities against transferable fishing rights.
It is equally important, for its sustainability, to take into account the access regime to waters, especially in waters or regions with biogeographic basins.

In the context of liberalization and the crisis affecting vulnerable regions, it must likewise be ensured that products originating from international trade comply with the same requirements as those imported by the Union.
Mr. President, Mr. President of the Board of Directors, Commissioner, I will be very brief, because I swear that what is at stake here is essentially a political issue, not a technical one.
A financial issue and the question at stake is the following: whether we want the European Union to make a difference in the context of the crisis, or whether we want to worsen the crisis.
Most European countries are today making a tremendous effort, in terms of their public finances, to overcome the crisis in a competitive manner, in a way that can position their countries
The European Union, on a global scale, is highly competitive and capable of competing effectively, but it is clear that without investment there can be no growth and this crisis cannot be overcome.
If the European Union insists on these drastic cuts, particularly in the cohesion policy and the common agricultural policy, what this will mean is that the countries which are making an enormous

Through this effort, they will not succeed in achieving growth, and this plan—which we have embraced in all countries for the European Union, sound finances with prospects for growth and competitiveness—will fail.

The sole and major instrument for this is precisely this multiannual financial framework, and therefore, if we wish for the European Union to succeed and for this policy to serve as an exemplary model for other countries, we must commit to the budget for the financial perspectives.
